question: A pendulum has a time period $T$ in air. When it is made to oscillate in water its time period becomes $\sqrt{2} T$. Then the relative density of the material of the bob of the pendulum is (neglect damping)
explanation:
The effective acceleration of a bob in water is given by,
${g^\prime } = g\left( {1 - \frac{\sigma }{\rho }} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Since, the periods of oscillation of bob in air and water are given as
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$ and $T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g^{\prime}}}$
We have, $\dfrac{T}{T^{\prime}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g^{\prime}}{g}}$
$\dfrac{T}{T^{\prime}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g\left(1-\dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}\right)}{g}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{T}{T^{\prime}}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac{1}{\rho}} \quad(\because \sigma=1)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{T}{T^{\prime}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac{1}{\rho}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2}=1-\dfrac{1}{\rho} \Rightarrow \rho=2$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: In a well the absolute pressure at a point $10\,m$ below the surface of water is ($g=10 {~ms}^{-2}$)
explanation:
Pressure $10\,m$ below the surface of water in a well will be
$P=P_{0}+\rho g h$
$=1.05 \times 10^{5}+1000 \times 10 \times 10=1.99 \times 10^{5} {~N} / {m}^{2}$
$=2 \times 10^{5} {~N} / {m}^{2}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: A cylindrical vessel, open at the top, contains 15 litres of water. Water drains out through a small opening at the bottom. 5 litre of water comes out in time $t_{1}$, the next 5 litre in further time $t_{2}$, and the last 5 litre in further time $t_{3}$,Then
explanation:
The situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958158/preptracker/questions/q_109842_explanation_1764958158263.png Velocity of efflux is given by
$v=\sqrt{2 g h}$
Let, for first $5\,L$ initial height is $h_{1}$
For next $5\,L$ height is $h_{2}$
For last $5\,L$ height is $h_{3}$
So, $h_{1}>h_{2}>h_{3}$
$\therefore \quad v_{1}>v_{2}>v_{3}$
As water flows out, the height of water level also decreases. This results in a decrease in water flow. $i.e.$ rate of water level decreases with time.
Therefore $t_{1} < t_{2} < t_{3}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: When a large bubble rises from the bottom of a lake to the surface, the volume of the bubble becomes 5 times its volume at the bottom of the lake. If $H$ is the atmospheric pressure expressed in terms of water column height, then the depth of the lake is (The temperature of the water in the lake is same at all points)
explanation:
Let the depth of water $=l$
So, pressure at depth $l$
$P=P_{0}+\rho g l$
$\Rightarrow P=\rho g(H+l)$
Since, surrounding temperature is constant, process take place isothermally.
Using Boyle's Law
${P_1}{V_1} = {P_2}{V_2}\left[ {{P_1} = \rho g(H + l),{P_2} = \rho gH} \right]$
Also ${V_2} = 5{V_1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( 1 \right)$
Put the value in Boyle's Law
${V_1}(\rho g(H) + \rho gl) = {V_2}(\rho gH)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From equation (1) and (2) we get
$l=4 H$
Hence, depth is $4\,H$.
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A swimming pool has a depth of $22\,m$ and area $700 {~m}^{2}$. Calculate the   fractional change $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}$ of water at the bottom of the swimming pool, given that the bulk modulus of water is $2 \cdot 2 \times 10^{9} {Nm}^{-2}, g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$, and density of water is $1000 {~kg} / {m}^{3}$.
explanation:
Hydrostatic pressure on the bottom of swimming pool
$P=\rho g h=1000 \times 10 \times 22 {~N} / {m}^{2}$
Now, we know that, the bulk modulus is given by
$B=\dfrac{-P}{\left(\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta V}{V}=-\dfrac{P}{B}=-\dfrac{1000 \times 10 \times 22}{2.2 \times 10^{9}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{|\Delta V|}{V}=10^{-4}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: A hollow spherical body of outer and inner radii of $4\,cm$ and $2\,cm$ respectively, floats half submerged in a liquid of density $2.0 {~g} / {cm}^{3}$. The density of the material of the sphere is
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952646/preptracker/questions/q_109835_explanation_1764952645759.png Weight of hollow sphere $=$ Buoyant force
$\Rightarrow \quad \rho^{\prime} \dfrac{4}{3} \pi\left[4^{3}-2^{3}\right] g=$ weight of displaced liquid
$\Rightarrow \rho^{\prime} \dfrac{4}{3} \pi[64-8] g=\rho \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi \times 4^{3} \times g$
$\Rightarrow \rho^{\prime} \times 56=\rho \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times 64$
$\Rightarrow \rho^{\prime}=2 \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{64}{56}=1.14 {~g} / {cm}^{3}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A $U$-shaped tube is partially filled with an incompressible liquid of density $1.2 {~g} / {cm}^{3}$. Oil which does not mix with the liquid is next poured into left side of the $U$-tube until the liquid rises by $15\,cm$ on the right side of $U$-tube. If the density of the oil is $0.9 {~g} / {cm}^{3}$, the oil level will stand higher than the liquid level of right side of $U$-tube by
explanation:
The situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952649/preptracker/questions/q_109826_explanation_1764952649032.png As we know that,
At same level, pressure remains same.
$\therefore P_{0}+\rho_{0} g(h+15)=P_{0}+\rho_{1} g(15+15)$
where, $P_{0}$ is atmospheric pressure.
$\therefore \rho_{0}(h+15)=\rho_{1} \times 30$
$\Rightarrow 0.9(h+15)=1.2 \times 30$
$\Rightarrow 3(h+15)=4 \times 30 \Rightarrow 3 h+45=120$
$\Rightarrow 3 h=75 \Rightarrow h=25 {~cm}$
$\therefore$ Difference in level of oil and liquid
$=25-15=10 {~cm}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: Consider a fluid in a container. Let the density of the fluid at the surface and at depth $H$ be $\rho_{0}$ and $\rho$, respectively. The bulk modulus of the fluid is $B_{0}$. If $\rho=\dfrac{\rho_{0}}{1+\alpha \rho_{0} g H}$, then the constant $\alpha$ is (Assume, $\left.\dfrac{\rho-\rho_{0}}{\rho_{0}} \ll 1\right)$
explanation:
We know that density of fluid at depth $d$
$\rho = \frac{{{\rho _0}}}{{1 + \alpha {\rho _0}gH}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$B_{0}=\dfrac{-P}{\Delta V / V}=\dfrac{-\rho g H}{\Delta V / V}$
Now from Eq. (1),
$\rho\left(1+\alpha \rho_{0} g H\right)=\rho_{0}$
$\Rightarrow \rho+\rho \alpha \rho_{0} g H=\rho_{0}$
$\Rightarrow \rho_{0}-\rho=\rho \alpha \rho_{0} g H$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta \rho }}{{{\rho _0}}} = \alpha \rho gH\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
and since $\rho=m / V$
Taking relative error both sides, we get
$\frac{{\Delta \rho }}{{{\rho _0}}} = \frac{{\Delta m}}{m} + \frac{{\Delta V}}{V}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
From Eqs. (2) and (3), we get
$\dfrac{\Delta m}{m}+\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}=\alpha \rho g H$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{\alpha}=\dfrac{\rho g H}{\Delta V / V} \quad\left[\because \dfrac{\Delta m}{m}=0\right]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{\alpha}=-B_{0} \Rightarrow \alpha=-\dfrac{1}{B_{0}}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: A wooden block of density $0.5 {~g} / {cc}$ is tied to a string. The other end of the string is fixed to the bottom of a tank. The tank is filled with a liquid of density $1 {~g} / {cc}$. If the tension in the string is $20\,N$, then the mass of the block is (Take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
The situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952648/preptracker/questions/q_109820_explanation_1764952647041.png As the block is floating
$\therefore F_{\text {net }}($ net force on $b l o c k)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad(m g)_{\text {Block }}+T-F_{\text {up }}=0$
$\Rightarrow(-m g)_{\text {Block }}+F_{\text {up }}=T$
$\Rightarrow-\rho_{B} g V_{B}+\rho_{0} g V_{B}=20 \times 10^{5}$
$\Rightarrow-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times V_{B}+1 \times 1000 V_{B}=20 \times 10^{5}$
$\Rightarrow 500\,V_{B}=2000 \times 10^{3}$
$\Rightarrow V_{B}=4 \times 10^{3} {~cm}^{3}$
As $\rho_{B}=m_{B} / V_{B}$
$\Rightarrow m_{B}=\rho_{B} V_{B}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 10^{3} g$
$=2 \times 10^{3} {~g}=2 {~kg}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A block of material with density $3 g / c c$ is placed on a fluid of density $7 {~g} / {cc}$. The  fraction of the volume of the block outside the fluid is
explanation:
Let the volume of the material be $V$ and the volume of the block submerged in the fluid is $n V$
$\therefore$ Weight of the block is $V \rho g$ and weight of the displaced fluid is $n V \sigma g$
According to the condition of equilibrium of a floating body.
$V \rho g=n V \sigma g \Rightarrow n=\dfrac{\rho}{\sigma}=\dfrac{3}{7}$
$\therefore$ The dfraction of volume of the block outside the fluid is
$1-n=1-\dfrac{3}{7}=\dfrac{4}{7}=0.57$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A cubical block of wood having mass of $160\,g$ has a metal piece fastened underneath as shown in the figure. Find the maximum mass of the metal piece which will allow the block to float in water. Specific gravity of wood is 0.8 and that metal is 10 and density of water $=1 {~g} / c c$.
explanation:
Volume of wood block
$=\dfrac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Density }}=\dfrac{160}{0.8 \times 1}=\dfrac{1600}{8}=200 {~cm}^{3}$
Let mass of metal piece is $x g$.
Volume of metal piece
$=\dfrac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Density }}=\dfrac{x}{10 \times 1}=\dfrac{x}{10} {~cm}^{3}$
Total volume of fluid displaced
$=\left(200+\dfrac{x}{10}\right) \times 10^{-6} m^{3}$
Weight of fluid displaced $=V \times \rho_{f} \times g$
$=\left(200+\dfrac{x}{10}\right) 10^{-6} \times 1000 \times 10 N$
$=\left(200+\dfrac{x}{10}\right) 10^{-2} N$
If block floats in water, then weight $=$ upthrust$\Rightarrow(160+x) 10 \times 10^{-3}=\left(200+\dfrac{x}{10}\right) \times 10^{-2}$
$160+x=200+\dfrac{x}{10}$
$\dfrac{9 x}{10}=40 \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{400}{9}=44.4 {~g}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A block of iron contains a cavity as shown below. The block weighs $6000\,N$ in air and 4000 $N$ in water. If the density of iron and water are $6 {~g} / {cm}^{3}$ and $1 {~g} / {cm}^{3}$, then the volume of the cavity is (assume, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
The block with cavity is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958150/preptracker/questions/q_109811_explanation_1764958150171.png According to figure given, volume of cavity,
$V_{1}=$ volume of water displaced $-$ volume of iron
$=\dfrac{\text { weight of water displaced }}{\rho_{\text {water }} \times g}-\dfrac{\text { weight of iron }}{\rho_{\text {iron }} \times g}$
$=\dfrac{6000-4000}{10^{3} \times 10}-\dfrac{6000}{6 \times 10^{3} \times 10} {~m}^{3}$
$\left[\begin{array}{l}\because \rho_{\text {iron }}=6 {~g} / {cm}^{3}=6 \times 10^{3} {~kg} / {m}^{3} \\ \rho_{\text {water }}=1 {~g} / {cm}^{3}=1 \times 10^{3} {~kg} / {m}^{3}\end{array}\right]$
$=0.2-0.1=0.1 {~m}^{3}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A hydraulic lift as shown in the figure is used to lift a mass of $1000\,kg$, which is placed on a piston $\left(P_{1}\right)$ of area $1 {~m}^{2}$. If the cross-section area of the piston $\left(P_{2}\right)$ at the other end is $0.01 {~m}^{2}$,then how much mass need to be put on it to lift the $1000\,kg$?
explanation:
According to Pascal's law, $P_{1}=P_{2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958145/preptracker/questions/q_109808_explanation_1764958145025.png $\Rightarrow \dfrac{F_{1}}{A_{1}}=\dfrac{F_{2}}{A_{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1000 g}{1}=\dfrac{m_{2} g}{0.01}$
$\left[\because A_{1}=1 m^{2}, A_{2}=0.01 m^{3}\right]$
$\Rightarrow m_{2}=1000 \times 0.01=10 {~kg}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force/Torque Exerted by a Static Liquid on a Surface and Hydraulic Machines

question: The tension in a massless cable connected to an iron ball of $100\,kg$ when it is submerged in sea water is ($\rho_{\text {iron }}=8 \times 10^{3} {~kg} / {m}^{3}$ and $\rho_{\text {sea water }}=1000 {~kg} / {m}^{3}, g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
When the ball is submerged in sea water, the upthrust force acting on the ball in upward direction. When the ball is completely submerged, it's effective weight gets decreased. This decrease in weight would be equal to the up thrust on the body according to Archimedes principle.
Hence tension $T$ in the massless cable is equal to the apparent weight on the ball.
$i.e.,$ $T=$ apparent weight
$T=W\left(1-\dfrac{\rho_{\text {sea water }}}{\rho_{\text {iron }}}\right)=m g\left(1-\dfrac{1000}{8 \times 10^{3}}\right)$
$=100 \times 10\left(1-\dfrac{1}{8}\right)=1000 \times \dfrac{7}{8}=875 {~N}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A cubical block of wood, of length $10\,cm$, floats at the interface between oil of density $800 {~kg} / {m}^{3}$ and water. The lower surface of the block is $1.5\,cm$ below the interface. If the depth of water is $10\,cm$ below the interface and oil is upto $10\,cm$ above the interface then the difference in pressure at the lower and the upper face of the wooden block is (Assume density of water, $\rho=1000 {~kg} / {m}^{3}$ and acceleration of gravity, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
The situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952640/preptracker/questions/q_109801_explanation_1764952639704.png If $V_{1}$ and $V_{2}$ be the volume of wooden block in water and oil respectively, then
$V_{1}: V_{2}=1.5: 8.5 \Rightarrow \dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\dfrac{1.5}{8.5}=\dfrac{3}{17}$
$\therefore \quad {V_1} = \frac{3}{{17}}{V_2}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\therefore V_{1}+V_{2}=V$, where, $V$ is the volume of the block.
From Eq. (1), we get
$\dfrac{3}{17} V_{2}+V_{2}=V \Rightarrow V_{2}=\dfrac{17}{20} {~V}$
$\therefore \quad V_{1}=\dfrac{3}{20} V$
Let $\rho$ be the density of the block.
Now, weight of block = weight of oil displaced $+$ weight of water displaced
$\Rightarrow V \rho g=V_{1} \rho_{w} g+V_{2} \rho_{\text {oil }} g$
$V \rho g=\dfrac{3}{20} V \rho_{w} g+\dfrac{17}{20} V \rho_{\text {oil }} g$
$\rho=\dfrac{3}{20} \rho_{w}+\dfrac{17}{20} \rho_{\text {oil }}=\dfrac{3}{20} \times 1000+\dfrac{17}{20} \times 800$
$=830 {~kg} / {m}^{3}$
$\therefore$ Pressure difference
$P=\rho g h=830 \times 10 \times 0.1=830 {~Pa}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: Consider a vessel filled with a liquid upto a height $H$. The bottom of the vessel lies in the $X Y$ -  plane passing through the origin. The density of the liquid varies with $Z$-axis as $\rho(z)=\rho_{0}\left[2-\left(\dfrac{z}{H}\right)^{2}\right]$.                   If $p_{1}$ and $p_{2}$ are the pressures at the bottom surface and top surface of the liquid, then the magnitude of ($p_{1}-p_{2}$) is
explanation:
Magnitude of $\left(P_{1}-P_{2}\right)$
$=g \times \int_{z=0}^{z=H} g \times d z \times \rho_{0}\left(2-\dfrac{z^{2}}{H^{2}}\right)$
$=g \times \int_{z=0}^{z=H} \rho_{0}\left(2-\dfrac{z^{2}}{H^{2}}\right) d z$
$=g \times\left[\rho_{0}\left(2 z-\dfrac{z^{3}}{3 H^{2}}\right)\right]_{z=0}^{z=H}$
$=g \rho_{0}\left(2 H-\dfrac{H^{3}}{3 H^{2}}\right)=g \rho_{0}\left(2 H-\dfrac{H}{3}\right)$
$=g \rho_{0}\left(\dfrac{5 H}{3}\right)=\dfrac{5}{3} \rho_{0} g h$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: A tank of height $15\,m$ and cross-section area $10 m^{2}$ is filled with water. There is a small hole of cross-section area $a$ which is much smaller than the area of cross-section of container, located at a height of $12\,m$ from the base of the container. How much force should be applied with a piston at the top level, so that the water coming out of the hole hits the ground at a distance of $16\,m$? (Take,$\text { density of water }=1000 {~kg} {~m}^{-3} \text { ) }$
explanation:
The situation is described below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952638/preptracker/questions/q_109797_explanation_1764952638078.png As, range of water jet $=16 {~m}$. If velocity of efflux is $v$, then
$v \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 H}{g}}=16$,
Also, $v=\sqrt{2 g h+\dfrac{2\left(P-P_{a}\right)}{\rho}}$,
where $P$ is the pressure just below the piston. So combining these, we get
$\left(2 g h+\dfrac{2\left(P-P_{a}\right)}{\rho}\right)\left(\dfrac{2 H}{g}\right)=256$
$\Rightarrow 2 \times 10 \times 3+\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{1000}=\dfrac{2560}{2 \times 12}$
$\Rightarrow \Delta P=23.3 {k} \dfrac{{N}}{{m}^{2}}$
So, force applied $=\Delta P \times A=233 k N$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force/Torque Exerted by a Static Liquid on a Surface and Hydraulic Machines

question: A sphere of relative density $\sigma$ and diameter $D$ has concentric cavity of diameter $d$. The ratio of $\dfrac{D}{d}$, if it just floats on water in a tank is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956076/preptracker/questions/q_39290_explanation_1764956076093.png
Relative density of sphere $=\sigma$
Diameter $=D$, Cavity diameter $=d$
Weight of sphere $(W)=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi \times \dfrac{1}{8}\left(D^{3}-d^{3}\right) \sigma g$
Buoyant force, $F_{B}=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi \dfrac{D^{3}}{8} \times 1 \times g$
For just floating, Weight = buoyant force
$\therefore \dfrac{4}{3} \pi \times \dfrac{1}{8}\left(D^{3}-d^{3}\right) \sigma g=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi \dfrac{D^{3}}{8} \times 1 \times g$
or $\left(D^{3}-d^{3}\right) \sigma=D^{3}$
or $1-\dfrac{d^{3}}{D^{3}}=\dfrac{1}{\sigma} \Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{D}{d}\right)^{3}=\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\sigma-1}\right)$
$\dfrac{D}{d}=\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\sigma-1}\right)^{1 / 3}$
subject: Physics

question: A cube of ice floats partly in water partly in kerosene oil. The ratio of volume of ice immersed in water to that in kerosene oil (specific gravity of kerosene oil $=0.8$, specific gravity of ice $=0.9$ )
explanation:
Weight of ice is balanced by buoyant force due to water and kerosene oil.
$\therefore m g=\rho_{k} V_{k} g+\rho_{w} V_{w} g$
$\rho_{\mathrm{ice}} V_{\mathrm{ice}} g=\rho_{k} V_{k} g+\rho_{w} V_{w} g$
$\dfrac{\rho_{\text {ice }}}{\rho_{w}} V_{\text {ice }}=\dfrac{\rho_{k}}{\rho_{w}} V_{k}+V_{w}$
$\Rightarrow 0.9 V_{\text {ice }}=0.8 V_{k}+V_{w}$
${({\rm{Volume}})_{{\rm{ice}}}} = {({\rm{Volume}})_{{\rm{water}}}} + {({\rm{Volume}})_{{\rm{kerosene}}}}$
$\therefore \quad 0.9\left(V_{w}+V_{k}\right)=0.8 V_{k}+V_{w}$
$0.1 V_{w}=0.1 V_{k} \Rightarrow \dfrac{V_{w}}{V_{k}}=\dfrac{1}{1}$
subject: Physics

question: A solid cylinder of height $h$ and mass $m$ is floating in a liquid of density $\rho$ as shown in the figure. Find the acceleration of the vessel  containing liquid for which the relative downward acceleration of the completely immersed cylinder w.r.t. vessel becomes equal to one-third of that of the vessel. (Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
Let the volume of the cylinder be $V$. When the cylinder is floating, up thrust $=$ weight. Hence, $\rho=\left(\dfrac{3}{4} V\right) g=m g \Rightarrow V=\dfrac{4 m}{3 \rho}$
Let the acceleration of the particle vessel be $A$ (upwards). In the reference frame of the vessel, the acceleration of the cylinder is $A \cdot 3$.
${\therefore \quad mg + mA - {\rm{up}}\,\,{\rm{trust}} = m\left( {\frac{A}{3}} \right)}$
${mg + mA - \rho V{g^\prime } = m\left( {\frac{A}{3}} \right)}$
where $g^{\prime}=g+A=$ effective value of $g$ for upthrust.
$\therefore \,\,mg + mA - \rho V(g + A) = m\left( {\frac{A}{3}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow mg - \frac{4}{3}m(g + A) = - m\frac{2}{3}A$
$A = \left( { - \frac{g}{2}} \right){\rm{ upwards }}$
The acceleration of the vessel should be $\dfrac{g}{2}=\dfrac{10}{2}=5 {~ms}^{-2}$(downwards).
subject: Physics

question: A uniform rod $A B, 12 {~m}$ long weighing 24 $kg$ , is supported at end $B$ by a flexible light string and a lead weight (of very small size) of 12 $kg$ attached at end $A$. The rod floats in water with one-half of its length submerged. Find the volume of the rod. [Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$, density of water $=1000 {~kg} / {m}^{3}$ ]
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957151/preptracker/questions/q_39210_explanation_1764957151301.png $T + U = {W_1} + {W_2} = 360\,N\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$U = \frac{V}{2}{\rho _w}g = (V/2)\left( {{{10}^3}} \right)(10)$
or $\quad U = 0.5 \times {10^4}\,V\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Taking torque about $M=0$
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & 120\left(\dfrac{l}{4}\right)+T\left(\dfrac{3 l}{4}\right)=240\left(\dfrac{l}{4}\right) \\\text { or } & T=\dfrac{240-120}{3}=40 {~N}=4 g\end{array}$
Now from Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$V=6.4 \times 10^{-2} {~m}^{3}=64 \times 10^{-3} {~m}^{3}$
subject: Physics

question: A tube of uniform cross-section has two vertical portions connected with a horizontal thin tube $8\,cm$ long at their lower ends. Enough water to occupy $22\,cm$ of the tube is poured into one branch and enough oil of specific gravity 0.8 to occupy $22\,cm$ is poured into the other. Find the distance  of the common surface ${E}$ of the two liquids from point ${B}$.
explanation:
${h_{{w}}+8 {~cm}+h_{0}=22 {~cm}+22 {~cm}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{ }}{h_w} + {h_0} = 36\,cm$
${P_C} = {P_B} \Rightarrow {h_0}{\rho _0}g = {h_w}{\rho _0}g$
$ \Rightarrow {h_0} \times 0.8 = {h_w} \times 1$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{ }}{h_0} = \frac{{{h_w}}}{{0.8}} = 1.25{h_w} \Rightarrow {h_w} + 1.25{h_w} = 36$
$ \Rightarrow {h_w} = \frac{{36}}{{2.25}} = 16\,cm \Rightarrow BE = 22 - 16 = 6\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: A ball of density ${\rho_{0}}$ falls from rest from a point ${P}$ onto the surface of a liquid of density ${\rho}$ in time ${T}$. It enters the liquid, stops, moves up, and returns to ${P}$ in a total time ${3 T}$. Neglect viscosity, surface tension and splashing. Find  the ratio of ${\dfrac{\rho}{\rho_{0}}}$.
explanation:
It strikes the surface of liquid with velocity ${v_{1}=g T}$ in water (liquid) its time of flight.
$T = \frac{{2{v_1}}}{{\left( {\frac{\rho }{{{\rho _0}}} - 1} \right)g}} = \frac{{2gT}}{{\left( {\frac{\rho }{{{\rho _0}}} - 1} \right)g}} \Rightarrow \frac{\rho }{{{\rho _0}}} = 3$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957076/preptracker/questions/q_39166_explanation_1764957075783.png
subject: Physics

question: The drawing shows a hydraulic chamber with a spring (spring constant ${=1600 {~N} / {m}}$) attached to the input piston and a rock of mass $40.0\,kg$ resting on the output plunger. The piston and plunger are nearly at the same height, and each has a negligible mass. By how much is the spring compressed  from its unstrained position?
explanation:
Let ${F_{1}}$ and ${F_{2}}$ are the magnitudes of the force the spring exerts on the piston and the rock exerts on the plunger respectively. Initially the piston and the plunger are at the same height, so the fluid pressure at the piston is equal to the fluid pressure at the plunger ${\dfrac{F_{1}}{A_{1}}=\dfrac{F_{2}}{A_{2}}}$ where ${A_{1}}$ is the area of the piston and ${A_{2}}$ is the area of the plunger.
Therefore, the magnitude of the force ${F_{2}}$ that the rock exerts on the plunger is given by ${F_{2}=F_{1}\left(\dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}\right)}$
Here ${F_{1}=k x}$, where ${k}$ is the spring constant of the spring, and ${x}$ is the amount by which the spring is compressed from its unstrained position. and the magnitude ${F_{2}}$ of the force the rock exerts on the plunger is equal to the magnitude ${W=m g}$ where ${m}$ is the rock's mass and ${g}$ is the magnitude of the acceleration due to gravity.
Solving ${F_{2}=F_{1}\left(\dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}\right)}$ for ${F_{1}}$ and substituting ${F_{2}=m g}$ yields ${F_1} = {F_2}\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}}} \right) = mg\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Solving for ${x}$, we obtain, $x = \frac{{{F_1}}}{k}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Substituting Eq. (1) into Eq. (2), we find that
$x = \frac{{{F_1}}}{k} = \frac{{mg}}{k}\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{(40\,kg)\left( {10\,m/{s^2}} \right)}}{{1600\,N/m}}\left( {\frac{{15\,c{m^2}}}{{75\,c{m^2}}}} \right) = 5.0 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force/Torque Exerted by a Static Liquid on a Surface and Hydraulic Machines

question:
Figure shows a cubical block of side $10\,cm$ and relative density 1.5 suspended by a wire of cross-sectional area ${10^{-6} {~m}^{2}}$. The breaking stress of the wire is ${7 \times 10^{6} {Nm}^{-2}}$. The block is placed in a beaker of base area ${200 {~cm}^{2}}$ and initially at ${t=0}$, the top surfaces of water and block coincide. There is a pump at the bottom corner, which ejects ${2 {~cm}^{3}}$ of water per second. If the time at which wire breaks is ${p \times 100}$ seconds, then the value of ${p}$ is
(Given, ${g=10 {~ms}^{-2}}$)
explanation:
${T_{\max }=\left(7 \times 10^{6}\right)\left(10^{-6}\right)=7 {~N}}$
Initial buoyant force is ${B_{F_{1}}=(0.1)^{3}(1000)(10)=10 {~N}}$
Weight of the block is ${w=(0.1)^{3}(1500)(10)=15 {~N}}$
${\therefore}$ Initial tension ${T_{i}=15-10=5 {~N}}$
At the time of breaking tension is ${T_{\max }=7 {~N}}$
${\therefore \quad B_{F_{\text {min }}}=15-7=8 {~N}}$ (at the time of breaking of wire)https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957045/preptracker/questions/q_39126_explanation_1764957044729.png ${\therefore \quad\left(V_{i}\right)(1000)(10)=8}$
${\Rightarrow \quad V_{i}=}$ volume immersed ${=8 \times 10^{-4} {~m}^{3}}$
Or ${V_{i}=800 {~cm}^{3}}$
Volume of water to be removed ${=(200-100)(2)=200 {~cm}^{3}}$
${\therefore 2 t=200}$
${\Rightarrow t=100 {~s}}$
${\Rightarrow p=1}$
subject: Physics

question:
A cube of wood of mass $0.5\,kg$ and density ${800 {~kg} {~m}^{-3}}$ is fastened to the free end of a vertical spring of spring constant ${k=50 {Nm}^{-1}}$, fixed at the bottom. Now, the entire system is completely submerged in water. The elongation or compression of the spring in equilibrium is ${\dfrac{\beta}{2} {~cm}}$. The value of ${\beta}$ is
(Given, ${g=10 {~ms}^{-2}}$)
explanation:
${\rho_{s} < \rho_{w}}$. So, block tends to move up and thus the spring has elongation.
${B_{F}=m g \times k x}$
${V \rho_{w} g=V \rho_{s} g+k x}$
${\dfrac{0.8}{800} \times 1000 \times 10=(0.5)(10)+50 x}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956067/preptracker/questions/q_39118_explanation_1764956067091.png ${x=0.025 {~m}=2.5 {~cm}=\dfrac{5}{2} {~cm}}$
${\Rightarrow \beta=5}$
subject: Physics

question: A ball whose density is $0.5 \times {10^3}\,kg{\rm{/}}{m^3}$ falls into water from a height of $10\,cm$. To what depth  does the ball sink?
explanation:
Velocity of ball before entering the water surface,$v = \sqrt {2gh} = \sqrt {2\,g \times 10} \,cm/s\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
When ball enters into water, due to upthrust of water the velocity of ball decreases.
$\therefore$ retardation, $\mathrm{a}=\dfrac{\text { apparent weight }}{\text { mass of ball }}$$ = \frac{{V(\sigma - \rho )g}}{{V\sigma }} = \left( {\frac{{\sigma - \rho }}{\sigma }} \right)g$
$ = \left( {\frac{{0.5 - 1}}{{0.5}}} \right) \times g$
$ = - g$
Let $d$ be the depth upto which ball sinks then, using III $^{\text {rd }}$ kinematical equation,
$0 - {v^2} = 2 \times - g \times d$
${{v^2} = 2gd}$
${\therefore \,\,\,\,\,2 \times g \times 10 = 2gd{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{[using}}\,\,{\rm{(1)]}}}$
${\therefore d = 10\,cm}$
subject: Physics

question: Two identical cylindrical vessels with their bases at the same level, contain oil of density $0.8\,g{\rm{/}}c{m^3}.$ The area of both vessels is $20\,c{m^2}$ but the height of liquid in one vessel is $10\,cm$ and in the other $20\,cm.$ When both cylinders are connected, the work done in joule (rounding of to $2$ decimal places) by gravity in equalising the levels is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956806/preptracker/questions/q_38960_explanation_1764956806119.png When cylinders are connected, levels in two vessels get equalised to $x = \frac{{10 + 20}}{2} = 15\,cm$ Volume of water that will be transferred to $BV$
$ = Ax = 20 \times 15 = 300\,c{m^3}$
This causes decrease in P.E.
$\therefore$ Decrease in P.E.$ = mgh$
$ = V\rho gx$
$ = 300 \times 0.8 \times 980 \times 15$
$ = 3.528 \times {10^6}erg$
Decrease in P.E. $=$ Work done $ = 3.528 \times {10^6}erg$
$ = 0.3528\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: A sphere of gold of radius $1\,cm$ and density $19.2\,g{\rm{/}}c{m^3}$ has a concentric spherical cavity. If it floats with its whole volume just immersed in molten aluminium of density $2.4\,g{\rm{/}}c{m^3}$ then volume of cavity in cubic centimetre would be   (Take $\pi=\dfrac{22}{7}$ )
explanation:
Sphere will float in liquid if its weight is less than or equal to weight of liquid displaced by it,
i.e., $\frac{4}{3}\pi \left( {{R^3} - {r^3}} \right)\sigma g \le \frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3}\rho g$
Where, $R$ is radius of sphere, $r$ is radius of cavity, $\sigma$ density of gold and $\rho$ is density of molten aluminium.As the sphere is floating with its whole volume just immersed in liquid,
$\left( {{R^3} - {r^3}} \right)\sigma = {R^3}\rho $
$\therefore \quad \frac{{{R^3} - {r^3}}}{{{R^3}}} = \frac{\rho }{\sigma } = \frac{{2.4}}{{19.2}}$
$\therefore 1 - \frac{{{r^3}}}{{{R^3}}} = \frac{1}{8}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{{{r^3}}}{{{R^3}}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8} = \frac{7}{8}$
$\therefore \quad {r^3} = \frac{7}{8} \times {1^3}$
$\therefore \quad$ Volume of cavity $=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}$$=\dfrac{4}{3} \times \dfrac{22}{7} \times \dfrac{7}{8} \times 1^{3}$
$\therefore \quad V = \frac{{11}}{3}\,c{m^3} = 3.67\,c{m^3}$
subject: Physics

question: A body of mass 2 $kg$ , volume ${0.001 {~m}^{3}}$ is in a fluid of density ${\dfrac{4}{3} {~g} / {cm}^{3}}$. The fluid at rest. The upward acceleration of the body in the fluid is (in ${{m} / {s}^{2}}$ )
explanation:
Density of body $(\rho )$
$=\dfrac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Volume }}=\dfrac{2}{0.001} {~kg} / {m}^{3}$
Density of fluid ( ${\sigma}$ )
$=\dfrac{4}{3} {~g} / {cm}^{3}=\dfrac{4}{3} \times 10^{3} {~kg} / {m}^{3}$
Weight of body ${(W)=V(\rho-\sigma)}$ g$=0.001\left(2-\dfrac{4}{3}\right) \times 10^{3} \times 10 {~N}=6.6 {~N}$
Downward acceleration
$=\dfrac{W}{m}=\dfrac{6.6}{2}=3.3 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
${\therefore}$ Upward acceleration ${=-3.3 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$.
So correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: A cube of mass ${m}$ and density ${D}$ is suspended in a liquid of density ${d < D}$ with the help of a spring of stiffness ${k}$. The elongation of spring is
explanation:
Let volume of cube ${=V}$
Weight of body in liquid ${=V g(D-d)}$$\begin{aligned}\therefore \quad k x & =V g(D-d)=\dfrac{m g}{D}(D-d) \\x & =\dfrac{m g}{k}\left(\dfrac{D-d}{D}\right) \\& =\dfrac{m g}{k}\left(1-\dfrac{d}{D}\right)\end{aligned}$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: Two miscible liquids of densities ${2.5 {~g} / {cm}^{3}}$ and ${0.8 {~g} / {cm}^{3}}$ are taken in the ratio ${2: 3}$ by mass and mixed. The average density of he resulting mixture is
explanation:
Let the mass of two liquids of densities ${2.5 {~g} / {cm}^{3}}$ and ${0.8 {~g} / {cm}^{3}}$ are $2\,mg$ and $3\,mg$ respectively
${V_{1}=\dfrac{m_{1}}{\rho_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{2 m}{2.5}\right) {cm}^{3}, V_{2}=\dfrac{m_{2}}{\rho_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{3 m}{0.8}\right) {cm}^{3}}$
Mass of resulting mixture ${=2 m+3 m=5 m {~g}}$
Volume of resulting mixture $ = {V_1} + {V_2}$
$ = \left( {\frac{{2m}}{{2.5}}} \right) + \left( {\frac{{3m}}{{0.8}}} \right) = \left( {\frac{{1.6 + 7.5}}{{2.5 \times 0.8}}} \right)mc{m^3}$
$ = 4.55m\,c{m^3}$
Average density of mixture ${=\left(\dfrac{5 m}{4.55 m}\right) {g} / {cm}^{3}}$
$\quad \quad \quad = 1.09\,g/c{m^3}.$
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: Two pieces of metals are suspended from the arms of a balance and are found to be in equilibrium when kept immersed in water. The mass of one piece is $32\,g$ and its density $8\,g\,c{m^{ - 3}}$. The density of the other is $5\,g$ per $c{m^3}$. Then the mass of the other is
explanation:
Volume of first piece of metal $ = \frac{{32}}{8} = 4\,c{m^3}$
Upthrust $ = 4\,gf$
Effective weight $ = (32 - 4)gf = 28gf$
If $m$ be the mass of second body, volume of second body is $\dfrac{m}{5}$
Now, $28 = m - \frac{m}{5} \Rightarrow m = 35\,g$
subject: Physics

question: A cube made of material having a density of  $0.9 \times {10^3}kg\,{m^{ - 3}}$ floats between water and a liquid of density $0.7 \times {10^3}kg\,{m^{ - 3}}$ which is immisicible with water. what part of the cube is immersed in water?
explanation:
Let $l=$ side of the cube,
$x=$ side of cube immersed in water
and $l-x=$ side of cube immersed in liquid.
According to law of floatation,
$\begin{array}{r}l^{3} \times 0.9 \times 10^{3} \times g=\left(l^{2} \times x\right) \times 1000 g \\+l^{2}(l-x) \times 0.7 \times 10^{3} g \\{\left[\because V_{1} \rho_{1} g=V_{2} \rho_{2} g\right]} \\\Rightarrow l \times 0.9=x+(l-x) \times 0.7\end{array}$
or $0.3 x=0.2 l$ or $\dfrac{x}{l}=\dfrac{2}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: An ice block floats in a liquid whose density is less than water. A part of block is outside the liquid. When whole of ice has melted, the liquid level will
explanation: Ice is lighter than water. When ice melts, the volume occupied by water is less than that of ice, due to which the level of water goes down.
subject: Physics

question: A liquid $X$ of density $3.36\,g/c{m^3}$ is poured in a  $U - $ tube in right arm with height $10\,cm,$ which contains $Hg.$ Another liquid $Y$ is poured in left arm with height $8\,cm.$ Upper levels of $X$ and $Y$ are same. What is the density of $Y$ ?
explanation:
As shown in figure, in the two arms of a tube atmospheric pressure remains same on surface $p p^{\prime}$. Hence,
$8 \times \rho_{Y} \times g+2 \times \rho_{H g} \times g$
$ = 10 \times {\rho _X} \times g$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956939/preptracker/questions/q_38799_explanation_1764956939399.png $\therefore \quad 8 \rho_{Y}+2 \times 13.6=10 \times 3.36$
or $\quad {\rho _Y} = \frac{{33.6 - 27.2}}{8} = 0.8\,g/cc$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: A $10\,c{m^3}$ cube floats in water with a volume of  $4\,c{m^3}$  remaining above the surface. The density of the material from which the cube is made, is
explanation:
According to Archimedes' principle
$\rho_{C} g V_{C}=\rho_{n} g V_{C S}$
where, $\rho_{C}$ and $\rho_{w}$ are densities of cube and water respectively; and $V_{C}$ and $V_{C S}$ are volumes of whole cube and submerged cube,respectively.
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{\text { Volume of cube submerged }}{\text { Total volume of cube }} \\& \quad=\dfrac{\text { Density of cube material }}{\text { Density of water }}\end{aligned}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{10 - 4}}{{10}} = \frac{d}{1}$
$ \Rightarrow d = \frac{6}{{10}} = 0.6\,g\,c{m^{ - 3}}$
subject: Physics

question: An ice-berg of density $900\,kg\,{m^{ - 3}}$ is floating in water of density $1000{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} kg\,{m^{ - 3}}.$ The percentage of volume of ice-berg outside the water is
explanation:
Let the volume of ice-berg is $V$ and its density is $\rho$. If this ice-berg floats in water with volume $V_{\text {in }}$ inside it, then
$\begin{aligned}& V_{\text {in }} \sigma g=V \rho g \\& \Rightarrow V_{\text {in }}=\left(\dfrac{\rho}{\sigma}\right) V\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}\Rightarrow V_{\text {out }} & =V-V_{\text {in }}=\left(\dfrac{\sigma-\rho}{\sigma}\right) V \\& =\left(\dfrac{1000-900}{1000}\right) V=\dfrac{V}{10}\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{V_{\text {out }}}{V}=0.1=10 \%$
subject: Physics

question: A solid floats with $1 / 4$ th of its volume above the surface of water, the density of the solid is
explanation:
Let $V$ and $\rho$ be volume and density of solid respectively and $\rho^{\prime}$ be the density of water,
i.e. $\quad \rho ' = {10^3}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} kg\,{m^{ - 3}}$
Weight of body $=V \rho g$
Volume of solid body outside water $=V / 4$
$\therefore$ Volume of solid body inside water$=V-V / 4=3 V / 4$
Weight of water displaced by solid
$=\dfrac{3 \mathrm{~V}}{4} \times 10^{3} \times g$
As solid body is floating, then
Weight of body $=$ Weight of water displaced by it
$V\rho g = \frac{{3V}}{4} \times {10^3}g$
$ \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{3}{4} \times 1000 = 750\,kg\,{m^{ - 3}}$
subject: Physics

question: Find density of ethyl alcohol.
explanation:
Pressure at left hand of a U-tube,
$p_{1}=p_{0}+\rho_{1} g h_{1}=p_{0}+\rho_{1} 10\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)$
or $\quad p_{1}=p_{0}+\rho_{1}$
Pressure at right hand of a U-tube,
$p_{2}=p_{0}+\rho_{2} g h_{2}=p_{0}+\rho_{2} 10\left(12 \times 10^{-2}\right)$
or $\quad {p_2} = {p_0} + 1.2\,\,{\rho _2}$
The mercury column in both arms of U-tube are at same level, therefore pressure in both arms will be same.
or $\quad p_{1}=p_{2} \Rightarrow p_{0}+\rho_{1}=p_{0}+1.2 \rho_{2}$
$\therefore$ Density of ethyl alcohol,
${\rho _2} = \frac{{{\rho _1}}}{{1.2}} = \frac{{1000}}{{1.2}} = 833.3\,kg\,{m^{ - 3}}$
$\quad \quad 0.83\,g\,c{m^{ - 3}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
Pressure in a reservoir of water is same at all points at the same level of water.
The pressure applied to enclosed water is transmitted in all directions equally.
explanation:
Pressure is same at all points at the same level in a reservoir because, pressure $=\rho g h$.
Where $\rho=$ Density of liquid,
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{g}} = $ Acceleration due gravity ,
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{h}} = $ Height of liquid.
As, $h$ is same, pressure will also be same.
According to Pascal's law, the pressure applied to enclosed water is transmitted in all directions equally.
Thus, both the statements are true.
So, correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B is correct.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.
Both Statement A and Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: A hydraulic automobile lift is designed to lift vehicles of mass $5000\,kg$. The area of cross - section of the cylinder carrying the load is $250\,c{m^2}$. The maximum pressure the smaller piston would have to bear is [Assume $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$].
explanation:
Pressure, $P=\dfrac{F}{A}$
Force, $F = mg = 5000 \times 10 = 50000\,N$
Area, $A = 250\,c{m^2} = 250 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{m^2}$
$\therefore$ Pressure on the piston is given by Pressure, $P=\dfrac{50000}{250 \times 10^{-4}}$
$ = 200 \times {10^4} = 2 \times {10^6}\,Pa$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force/Torque Exerted by a Static Liquid on a Surface and Hydraulic Machines

question: An air bubble of volume $1\,c{m^3}$ rises from the bottom of a lake $40\,m$ deep to the surface at a temperature of $12^\circ C$. The atmospheric pressure is $1 \times {10^5}\,Pa$, the density of water is $1000\,kg/{m^3}$ and $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$. There is no difference in the temperature of water at the depth of $40\,m$ and on the surface. The volume of air bubble when it reaches the surface will be
explanation:
Given, volume of bubble at depth of $40\,m = 1\,c{m^3}$
Depth of lake,
$d = 40\,m,T = 12^\circ C$
${P_1} = 1 \times {10^5}\,Pa$;
Density of water $ = 1000\,kg/{m^3};g = 10\,m/{s^2}$ As temperature is constant at depth and on the surface, $P V=$ constant
$\therefore P_{1} V_{1}=P_{2} V_{2}$
$P_{1} V_{1}=\left(P_{1}+h \rho g\right) V_{2}$
$1 \times {10^5} \times {V_1} = \left( \begin{array}{l} 1 \times {10^5} + 40 \times \\ 1000 \times 10 \end{array} \right) \times {10^{ - 6}}$
$V_{1}=\dfrac{5 \times 10^{5} \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{5}}$
${V_1} = 5 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,{m^3} = 5\,c{m^3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark the correct options given below:

Assertion: When you squeeze one end of a tube to get toothpaste out from the other end, Pascal's principle is observed.

Reason: A change in the pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of its container.
explanation: According to Pascal's law, pressure applied to an enclosed fluid, will transmitted without a change in magnitude to every point of the fluid and also to the walls of container. Due to that when any part of tooth paste's tube is squeezed, toothpaste squirts out and pressure applied is equally transmitted throughout the tube and when pressure reaches the open end, it forces toothpaste out through the opening. So, its one of the application of Pascal's law. Correct option is (2).

options.text: Assertion is not correct but Reason is correct.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct ans Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is not correct.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question:
Assertion: In a pressure cooker, the water is brought to boil. The cooker is then removed from the stove. Now on removing the lid of the pressure cooker, the water starts boiling again.

Reason: The impurities in water bring down its boiling point.
explanation: We know that the boiling point of a liquid increases with an increase in vapor pressure. In a cooker, water with the food to be cooked is heated, raising the pressure due to confined water vapors. Consequently, water boils at a temperature higher than its standard boiling point of $100^\circ C$. Now, when the cooker is removed from the stove and its lid is opened, the pressure decreases, causing the boiling point to return to $100^\circ C$, and water starts boiling again. When we add small impurities which are solid to water, the boiling point increases. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The shape of an automobile is so designed constant with that its front resembles the streamline pattern of the fluid through which it moves.

Reason: The resistance offered by the fluid is maximum in streamlined shapes.
explanation: When a body moves through a fluid, it experiences resistance from fluid friction, and this resistance increases with the body's speed. In the case of cars and aeroplanes moving through the air, they encounter air friction, which is influenced by the shape of the body. To minimize air friction, cars and planes are designed with streamlined shapes. These streamlined shapes allow for the smooth movement of air layers over the upper and lower surfaces of the body, creating lift and ultimately increasing the speed of the vehicle. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A dam for water reservoir is built thicker at the bottom than at the top.

Reason: Pressure of water is very large at the bottom.
explanation: In a dam the pressure exerted by the water increases with depth. Bottom is constructed with more materials and thickness to ensure stability. So correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: Sudden fall of pressure at a place indicates a storm.

Reason: Air flows from higher pressure to lower pressure.
explanation: A sudden fall in pressure indicates "an approaching storm," as air tends to flow from regions of higher pressure to lower pressure, which can lead to atmospheric disturbances and weather changes associated with storms. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: An air bubble comes from the bottom of a lake to the top.

Reason: Radius of bubble decreases in above process.
explanation:
Fluid, pressure increases with depth, the pressure $(P)$ at the top $(o)$ will be lower than that at the bottom $(p)$, given by the formula $P=P_{o}+\rho g h$, where $\rho$ is density, $g$ is a acceleration due to gravity, and $\mathrm{h}$ is depth. As a result, an air bubble moves from the bottom to the top and not sideways.
By Boyle's law, $P V=$ Constant. Therefore, as pressure decreases, volume increases, meaning the radius of the bubble increases. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A piece of ice floats in water. The level of water remains unchanged when the ice melts completely.

Reason: According to Archimede's principle, the loss in weight of the body in the liquid is equal to the weight of the liquid displaced by the immersed part of the body.
explanation: By Archimedes' principle, the loss in weight of the ice (due to melting) in the liquid is equal to the weight of the liquid displaced by the immersed part of the ice. The volume of water displaced by the ice is exactly the same as the volume of water produced when the ice melts. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A man sitting in a boat which is floating on a pond. If the man drinks some water from the pond, the level of the water in the pond remain unchanged.

Reason: According to Archimede's principle the weight of water displaced by body is equal to the weight of the body, if the body is floating.
explanation: This is in accordance with Archimedes' principle, which states that the weight of the water displaced by the boat (and the man) is equal to the weight of the boat and man. Therefore, removing a small amount of water from the pond does not significantly affect the water level. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: Hydrostatic pressure is a scalar quantity

Reason: Pressure is a type of force.
explanation:
Pressure $=\left(\dfrac{\text { Force }}{\text { Area }}\right)$
An applied force on a liquid results in the equal transmission of pressure in all directions within the liquid, hydrostatic pressure does not possess a fixed direction. Hydrostatic pressure is a scalar quantity. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: The blood pressure in humans is greater at the feet than at the brain.

Reason: Pressure of liquid at any point is proportional to height. Density of liquid and acceleration due to gravity.
explanation: Pressure of a liquid at any point is determined by the height of the liquid column, its density, and the acceleration due to gravity ( $P=h \rho g$ ). In a standing person, the blood has to pump against gravity to reach the brain. There is higher blood pressure at the feet. So correct option is (1).

question:
Assertion: Pascal's law is the working principle of a hydraulic lift.

Reason: Pressure is equal to thrust.
explanation:
By Pascal's law, (if gravity effect is neglected), the pressure at every point of liquid in equilibrium of rest is same in hydraulic lift Small force ( $\left.F_{1}\right)$ applied on the smaller piston (of area $a_{1}$ ) will be appearing as a very large force $\left(F_{2}\right)$ on the larger piston (of area $a_{2}$ ).
$P_{1}=P_{2}$ i.e., $\dfrac{F_{1}}{a_{1}}=\dfrac{F_{2}}{a_{2}}$ or, $F_{2}=\dfrac{a_{1}}{a_{2}} F_{1}$
As, $a_{2} \gg a_{1}$
$\therefore F_{2} \gg F_{1}$
But pressure is not thrust. It is force per unit area. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: The total weight of a piece of wood is $6\,kg$. In the floating state in water its $\dfrac{1}{3}$ part remains inside the water. On this floating piece of wood what maximum weight is to be put such that the whole of the piece of wood is to be drowned in the water?
explanation:
Weight of submerged part of the block
$W=\dfrac{1}{3} v$ (Density of water) $g$
Excess weight, $=$ weight of water having $\dfrac{2}{3}$ volume of the block.
$W^{\prime}=\dfrac{2}{3} v$ (Density of water) $g$
Dividing (2) by (1)
$\frac{{W'}}{W} = \frac{{2/3}}{{1/3}}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \therefore W' = 2\,W$
$ \Rightarrow W' = 2 \times 6 = 12\,kg$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A ship floats higher in the water on a high pressure day than on a low pressure day.

Reason: Floating of ship in the water is not possible because of buoyancy force which is present due to pressure difference.
explanation:
Variations in atmospheric pressure do not significantly impact the level at which a ship floats. Assertion is false.
Ships float in water due to buoyancy, which is a result of the weight of the water displaced by the ship being equal to the weight of the ship itself. Atmospheric pressure is not a direct factor in the buoyancy of a ship.
So, correct option is (4)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and Reason are incorrect.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Taking into account the fact that any object which floats must have an average density less than that of water, during world war $I$, a number of cargo vessels were made of concrete.

Reason: Concrete cargo vessels were filled with air.
explanation:
The assertion is correct because concrete cargo vessels were indeed constructed during World War I.
This reason is also true. But, the mere fact that concrete cargo vessels were filled with air doesn't directly explain why they floated. The correct explanation should emphasize the principle of buoyancy, which relates to the difference in density between the vessel and the water it displaces.
So, correct option is (2).

question: A long cylindrical vessel is half filled with a liquid. When the vessel is rotated about its own vertical axis, the liquid rises up near the wall. If the radius of vessel is $5\,cm$ and its rotational speed is 2 rotations per second, then the difference in the heights between the centre and the sides, in $$\mathrm{cm}$$, will be
explanation:
The linear speed of the liquid at the sides is $$r \omega$$. At the centre of the liquid and on the top surface the pressure is equal to atmospheric pressure. $$\rho \dfrac{r^{2} \omega^{2}}{2}=\rho g h$$
$2gh = {\omega ^2}{r^2}$
$ \Rightarrow h = \frac{{{{(2 \times 2\pi )}^2}{{\left( {5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)}^2}}}{{2 \times 10}} \approx 0.02\,m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure and Level of Liqiud Surface Inside an Accelerating/Rotating Container

question: A thin uniform tube is bent into a circle of radius $$r$$ in the vertical plane. Equal volumes of two immiscible liquids, whose densities are $$\rho_{1}$$ and $$\rho_{2}\left(\rho_{1}>\rho_{2}\right)$$, fill half the circle. The angle $$\theta$$ between the radius vector passing through the common interface and the vertical is:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956024/preptracker/questions/q_38037_explanation_1764956023990.png
Equating the pressure at $$\mathrm{G}$$ from both sides
$$\begin{aligned}& \rho_{1} g(R-R \sin \theta)=\rho_{1} g(R-R \cos \theta)+\rho_{2} g R \sin \theta \\& +\rho_{2} g R \cos \theta \\& \rho_{1} g R(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)=\rho_{2} g R(\cos \theta+\sin \theta) \\& \rho_{1}(1-\tan \theta)=\rho_{2}(1+\tan \theta) \\& \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\rho_{1}-\rho_{2}}{\rho_{1}+\rho_{2}}\right)\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: Two ice cubes of side \[10\;cm\], having cavity of volume \[20\;c{m^3}\] at centre of cube but filled with different materials \[A\] and \[B\] respectively. The specific gravity of material \[A\] is 4.9 and specific gravity of material B is 1.9. Now these cubes are placed in two different vessels of same base area as shown in figure. The water level before putting blocks in vessels are same. Assume that ice melts uniformly from all sides and with same constant rate in both the vessels (specific gravity of ice $$=0.9$$ ) Choose the correct graph showing the variation of heights of waterlevel in two vessels with time:
explanation:
Specific gravity of $A$ is more than that of $B$.
$$\Rightarrow h_{1}>h_{2}$$
When the ice starts melting the level of water remain constant in both containers. After some time the level decreases in both containers and finally levels will be equal when both the materials $A$ and $B$ sink.
subject: Physics

question: A cubical wooden block of side $$L$$ and relative density 0.5 is floating on the surface of water as shown in the figure. A string is attached by pulley to it as shown in figure. The graph of tension \[T\] in the string vs distance of the bottom of the block from the free surface of the water when the end \[A\] of the rope is slowly being pulled up will be (block is moving very slowly):
explanation: Initially the cube is floating so buoyancy force is equal to weight and tension in the string is zero. When the block is pulled inside the water the buoyancy force increases linearly and hence tension also increases. When the block completely submerges in water the tension in the string remain constant.
subject: Physics

question: A uniform cylinder of length $$L$$ and mass $$M$$ having cross-sectional area $A$ is suspended, with its length vertical, from a fixed point by a massless spring such that it is half submerged in a liquid of density $$\sigma$$ at equilibrium position. The extension $$x_{0}$$ of the spring when it is in equilibrium is:
explanation:
From figure, $$k x_{0}+F_{B}=M g$$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956165/preptracker/questions/q_37941_explanation_1764956164903.png
$$k x_{0}+\sigma \dfrac{L}{2} A g=M g$$
$$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow k x_{0}=M g-\sigma \dfrac{L}{2} A g \\& \Rightarrow x_{0}=\dfrac{M g-\dfrac{\sigma L A g}{2}}{k}=\dfrac{M g}{k}\left(1-\dfrac{L A \sigma}{2 M}\right)\end{aligned}$$
Hence, extension of the spring when it is in equilibrium is, $$x_{0}=\dfrac{M g}{k}\left(1-\dfrac{L A \sigma}{2 M}\right)$$
subject: Physics

question: In the arrangement as shown, $$m_{B}=3 m$$, density of liquid is $$\rho$$ and density of block $B$ is $$2 \rho$$. The system is released from rest so that block $$B$$ moves up when it is inside the liquid and moves down when it is outside of liquid with the same magnitude of acceleration. Find the mass of block $A$.
explanation:
Acceleration when block $$B$$ is in the liquid
$$\begin{aligned}& a_{1}=\dfrac{m_{A} g-\left(m_{B} g-\text { upthrust }\right)}{\left(m_{A}+3 m\right)} \\& =\dfrac{m_{A} g-\left(3 m g-\dfrac{3 m}{2 \rho} \rho g\right)}{\left(m_{A}+3 m\right)}(\uparrow)\end{aligned}$$
Accelaration when block $B$ is outside of the liquid
$$a_{2}=\dfrac{-m_{A} g+(3 m g)}{\left(m_{A}+3 m\right)}(\downarrow)$$
Given $$a_{1}=a_{2}$$, we get
$$\begin{aligned}& m_{A} g-\dfrac{3}{2} m g=3 m g-m_{A} g \\& \Rightarrow 2 m_{A} g=\dfrac{9}{2} m g \Rightarrow m_{A}=\left(\dfrac{9}{4}\right) m\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: A wooden rod of uniform cross-section and of length $120\,cm$ is hinged at the bottom of the tank which is filled with water to a height of 40 $cm$. In the equilibrium position, the rod makes an angle of $$60^{\circ}$$ with the vertical. The centre of buoyancy is located on the rod at a distance (from the hinge) of :
explanation:
OP is the portion of the rod immersed in water.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956157/preptracker/questions/q_37933_explanation_1764956157318.png
$\cos 60^\circ = \frac{{40}}{{OP}}$
$ \Rightarrow OP = \frac{{40}}{{\cos 60^\circ }}\,cm = \frac{{40}}{{1/2}}\,cm = 80\,cm$
The centre of buoyancy is at the centre of the immersed part of the rod. So, the required distance is $40\,cm$.
subject: Physics

question: A sealed tank containing a liquid of density $$\rho$$ moves with a horizontal acceleration $$a$$, as shown in the figure. The difference in pressure between the points $A$ and $B$ is
explanation:
Consider an element of the liquid of width $$d x$$ and area of cross-section $$\alpha$$, at a distance $$x$$ from the front of the tank.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956943/preptracker/questions/q_37929_explanation_1764956943226.png
Mass of the element $$=d m=(\alpha d x) \rho$$.
Net force to the right on the element
$$=(p+d p) \alpha-p \alpha=\alpha d p$$
From Newton's $$2^{\text {nd }}$$ law
$$\therefore \alpha d p=(\rho \alpha d x) a$$
$$d p=\rho a d x$$
$$\int_{A}^{C} d p=\int_{A}^{C} \rho a d x \text { or } p_{C}-p_{A}=\rho a l$$
(Refer to the figure in the question to identify $A$, $B,C)$
$$\begin{aligned}& \text { Also } p_{B}-p_{C}=\rho g h \\& \begin{aligned}p_{B}-\left(p_{A}+\rho a l\right)=\rho g h \\p_{B}-p_{A}=h \rho g+l \rho a\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure and Level of Liqiud Surface Inside an Accelerating/Rotating Container

question: The acceleration $$a$$ of the vertical $$U$$-tube is
explanation: $$\tan \theta=\dfrac{3 b-b}{4 b}=\dfrac{a}{g} \Rightarrow a=\dfrac{g}{2}$$
subject: Physics

question: A rectangular box containing water is accelerated upward at $3\,m/{s^2}$ on an inclined plane making $$30^{\circ}$$ to the horizontal. The slope of the free liquid surface is
explanation:
The pseudo force components $$m a \cos 30^{\circ}$$ $$\& m a \sin 30^{\circ}$$ are shown for a liquid element on the surface.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956941/preptracker/questions/q_37921_explanation_1764956941176.png
The surface of the liquid is always perpendicular to resultant force
$$\tan \theta=\dfrac{a_{x}}{a_{y}+g}=\dfrac{a \cos 30^{\circ}}{a \sin 30^{\circ}+g}=\dfrac{\dfrac{3 \times \sqrt{3}}{2}}{\dfrac{3}{2}+9.8}=0.23$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure and Level of Liqiud Surface Inside an Accelerating/Rotating Container

question: An astronaut is standing at the north pole of a newly discovered planet of radius, $$R$$. He holds a container full of liquid having mass $$m$$ and volume, $V$. At the surface of the liquid pressure is $$P_{0}$$ and at a depth $d$ below the surface, pressure is $P$. From this information determine the mass of the planet is
explanation:
$$\rho g d=\left(p-p_{0}\right)$$
$$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow g=\dfrac{\left(p-p_{0}\right)}{d\left(\dfrac{m}{V}\right)} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{G M}{R^{2}}=\dfrac{\left(p-p_{0}\right) V}{d m} \\& \Rightarrow M=\dfrac{\left(p-p_{0}\right) V R^{2}}{G d m}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: The $U$-tube shown has a uniform cross-section. A liquid is filled in the two arms upto heights $$h_{1}$$ and $$h_{2}$$ and then the liquid is allowed to move. Neglect viscosity and surface tension. When the levels equalize in the two arms, the liquid will
explanation:
From the above problem we can write the decrease in gravitational potential energy as
$\Delta U = {\left( {\frac{{{h_1} - {h_2}}}{2}} \right)^2}A\rho g\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
The mass of the entire liquid $$=\left(h_{1}+h_{2}+h\right) A \rho$$
If this moves with a velocity $$v$$,
Kinetic energy $ = \frac{1}{2}\left( {{h_1} + {h_2} + h} \right)A\rho {v^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq's (1) & (2)
$$v=\left(h_{1}-h_{2}\right) \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{2\left(h_{1}+h_{2}+h\right)}}$$

options.text:
Be moving with a velocity of
$$\left(h_{1}-h_{2}\right) \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{2\left(h_{1}+h_{2}+h\right)}}$$
Exert a net force to the right on the tube

subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: A tube in vertical plane is shown in figure. It is filled with a liquid of density $$\rho$$ and its end $B$ is closed. Then the force exerted by the fluid on the tube at end $B$ will be: [Neglect atmospheric pressure and assume the radius of the tube to be negligible in comparison to $$l]$$
explanation: $$F=\Delta P \cdot A=\rho g l A_{0}$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force/Torque Exerted by a Static Liquid on a Surface and Hydraulic Machines

question: A triangular lamina of area $$A$$ and height $$h$$ is immersed in a liquid of density $$\rho$$ in a vertical plane with its base touching the surface of the liquid. The pressure force on one surface of the lamina is
explanation:
Thrust on
lamina $$=$$ pressure at centroid $$\times$$ Area
$$=\dfrac{h \rho g}{3} \times A=\dfrac{1}{3} A \rho g h$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force/Torque Exerted by a Static Liquid on a Surface and Hydraulic Machines

question: Two solid bodies form a balance and the arm is in horizontal position. The mass of one body is $36\,g$ and its density is $9\,g/c{m^3}$. If the mass of the other is $48\,g$, its density in $g/c{m^3}$ is
explanation:
Let $T$ be the tension in the string.
$$\begin{aligned}& T=m g-F_{b}=m g-m_{\ell} g=\left(m-\rho_{\ell} V\right) g \\& \rho_{\ell}=\text { density of water. }\end{aligned}$$
As the system is in equilibrium
${T_1} = {T_2}$
$\left( {{m_1} - {\rho _\ell }{V_1}} \right)g = \left( {{m_2} - {\rho _\ell }{V_2}} \right)g$
$36 - 1 \times \frac{{36}}{9} = 48 - 1 \times \frac{{48}}{{{\rho _2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\rho _2} = 3\,g/c{m^3}$
subject: Physics

question: A solid cone of height $$H$$ and base radius $$H / 2$$ floats in a liquid of density $$\rho$$. It is hanging from the ceiling with the help of a string. The force by the fluid on the curved surface of the cone is ( $$P_{0}=$$ atmospheric pressure)
explanation:
Buoyancy force $$=$$ Net pressure force
$$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{1}{3} \pi\left(\dfrac{H}{2}\right)^{2} H \rho g & =\left(P_{0}+\rho g H\right) \pi\left(\dfrac{H}{2}\right)^{2}-F_{v} \\& =P_{0} \dfrac{\pi H^{2}}{4}+\dfrac{\rho g H^{3} \pi}{6}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: A hemispherical portion of radius $$R$$ is removed from the bottom of a cylinder of radius $R$. The volume of the remaining cylinder is $V$ and its mass $M$. It is suspended by a string in a liquid of density $$\rho$$ where it stays vertical. The upper surface of the cylinder is at a depth $$h$$ below the liquid surface. The force on the bottom of the cylinder by the liquid is:
explanation:
Let $$F_{b}(=\rho V g)$$ is the buoyancy force on the cylinder. Let $$F$$ is the pressure force exerted by the liquid on the base of the cylinder.
From the definition of buoyancy force
$$\begin{aligned}& F_{b}=F-\pi R^{2} \rho g h \\& \rho V g=F-\pi R^{2} \rho g h \\& \Rightarrow \quad F=\rho g V+\pi R^{2} \rho g h\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: If level of the liquid starts decreasing slowly when the level of liquid is at a height $$h_{1}$$ above the cylinder the block starts moving up. At what value of $$h_{1}$$, will the block rise:
explanation:
At equilibrium the pressure force on the block is balanced by its weight
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955945/preptracker/questions/q_37846_explanation_1764955944624.png
Here the effect of atmospheric pressure is not considered since $$P_{a}$$ effect is there both on top $$\&$$ base of the block. $$F_{P_{1}}$$ and $$F_{P_{2}}$$ are pressure forces.
$$\begin{aligned}& F_{P_{1}}+F_{g}=F_{P_{2}} \\& \rho g h_{1} \pi(2 r)^{2}+\dfrac{\rho}{3} g H \pi(2 r)^{2} \\& =\rho g\left(h_{1}+H\right) \pi\left[(2 r)^{2}-r^{2}\right] \\& h_{1}=\dfrac{5 H}{3}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: A hollow cylinder of mass $m$ made heavy at its bottom is floating vertically in water. It is tilled from its vertical position through an angle $$\theta$$ and is left. The restoring force acting on it is
explanation:
Let $$l$$ be the length of the cylinder, present inside water initially. Let $A$ be the cross-sectional area of the cylinder. Equating weight of the cylinder with the upthrust, we get
$mg = Alpg \Rightarrow m = Alp$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955943/preptracker/questions/q_37842_explanation_1764955943028.png
When the cylinder is tilted through an angle $$\theta$$, length of cylinder in water $$=\dfrac{l}{\cos \theta}$$
Weight of water displaced $$=\dfrac{l}{\cos \theta} A \rho g$$
Restoring force $$=\dfrac{l A \rho g}{\cos \theta}-l A \rho g$$
$$=\operatorname{lA\rho g}\left[\dfrac{1}{\cos \theta}-1\right]=m g\left[\dfrac{1}{\cos \theta}-1\right]$$
subject: Physics

question:
A uniform solid cylinder of density $0.8\,g/c{m^3}$ floats in equilibrium in a combination of two nonmixing liquids $$A$$ and $$B$$ with its axis vertical. The densities of the liquids $A$ and $B$ are $0.7\,g/c{m^3}$ and $1.2\,g/c{m^3}$ respectively. The height of liquid $A$ is ${h_A} = 1.2\,cm$. The length of the cylinder immersed in liquid $B$ is ${h_B} = 0.8\,cm$. Cross sectional area $ = 1\,c{m^2}$
Atmospheric pressure $ = {10^5}\,N/{m^2}$
Find $$h$$, the length of the part of the cylinder in air.
explanation:
As the cylinder is in equilibrium $$F_{g}=F_{b}$$ $$0.8(h+1.2+0.8)=1.2 \times 0.7+0.8 \times 1.2$$
$ \Rightarrow h = 0.25\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: On a smooth inclined plane, making an angle $$\alpha$$ with horizontal, a trolley containing a liquid of density $$\rho$$ slicles down. What is the angle of inclination $$\theta$$ of free surface with horizontal?
explanation:
The force $$m g$$ and pseudo force $$m a$$ act on a liquid particle on the surface as shown in the figure
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956930/preptracker/questions/q_37834_explanation_1764956930502.png
As the trolley moves down with acceleration $$g \sin \alpha$$ on the inclined plane so $$a=g \sin \alpha$$. The pseudo force $$m a(=m g \sin \alpha)$$ is balanced by $$m g \sin \theta$$ (gravitational force component). The only unbalanced force is $$m g \cos \alpha$$. A liquid surface is always present perpendicular to net force so surface of the liquid is parallel to the inclined plane. So $$\theta=\alpha$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure and Level of Liqiud Surface Inside an Accelerating/Rotating Container

question: A narrow tube completely filled with a liquid is lying on a series of tyres as shown in figure. Assuming no sliding between any surfaces, the value of acceleration of the cylinders for which liquid will not come out of the tube from anywhere is given by
explanation:
If the liquid does not come out then the pressure exerted by vertical and horizontal columns must be balanced at the bending point.
$$(\rho a L) A=(\rho g H) A$$
$$a=\dfrac{g H}{L}$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure and Level of Liqiud Surface Inside an Accelerating/Rotating Container

question: Two identical cylindrical vessels with their bases at the same level, each contains a liquid of density $$\rho$$. The height of the liquid in one vessel is $$h_{1}$$ and that in the other $$h_{2}$$. The area of either base is $A$. The work done by gravity in equalizing the levels when the vessels are inter connected is
explanation:
The center gravity of liquid in the first column is at a height $$\dfrac{h_{1}}{2}$$ and that in the second column is $$\dfrac{h_{2}}{2}$$
Let initial Potential Energy be $U$,
$$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow U_{1}=\left(A h_{1} \rho\right) g\left(\dfrac{h_{1}}{2}\right)+\left(A h_{2} \rho\right) g\left(\dfrac{h_{2}}{2}\right) \\& \Rightarrow U_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} A \rho g\left(h_{1}^{2}+h_{2}^{2}\right)\end{aligned}$$
When the water column in the two equalises then equivalent height is $$\dfrac{h_{1}+h_{2}}{2}$$ and the center of gravity in both the columns is $$\dfrac{h_{1}+h_{2}}{4}$$.
If $$U_{2}$$ be the final potential energy in both the columns then
${U_2} = 2\left[ {A\left( {\frac{{{h_1} + {h_2}}}{2}} \right)\rho \left( {\frac{{{h_1} + {h_2}}}{4}g} \right)} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow {U_2} = \frac{1}{4}A\rho g{\left( {{h_1} + {h_2}} \right)^2}$
Since work done is equal to the decrease in potential energy.
So, $$W=U_{1}-U_{2} \Rightarrow W=A \rho g\left(\dfrac{h_{1}-h_{2}}{2}\right)^{2}$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pressure in a Static Fluid/Pascal’s Law

question: A body of density $$\rho$$ is dropped from rest from a height $h$ into a lake of density $$\sigma(\sigma>\rho)$$. The maximum depth the body sinks inside the liquid is (neglect viscous effect of liquid)
explanation:
$v = \sqrt {2gh} $
Retardation in liqiud $a = \frac{{V\sigma g - V\rho g}}{V} = \left( {\frac{{\sigma - \rho }}{\rho }} \right)g$
$\therefore {d_{{\text{max}}}} = \frac{{{v^2}}}{{2a}} = \frac{{2gh}}{{2 - \frac{{\rho h}}{{\pi - \rho }}}} = \frac{{\rho h}}{{\sigma - \rho }}$
subject: Physics

question: A raft of mass $M = 600\,kg$ floats in calm water with $7\,cm$ submerged. When a man stands on the raft, $8.4\,cm$ are submerged, the man's mass is
explanation:
Upthrust on $7\,cm = 600\,kg$
$$\therefore$$ Upthrusty on further $1.4\,cm = \frac{{600}}{7} \times 1.4 = 120\,kg$
subject: Physics

question: A body measures $5\,N$ in air and $2\,N$ when put in water. The buoyant force is
explanation: $F = {W_{{\text{air }}}} - {W_{{\text{water }}}}$ or $\Delta W$
subject: Physics

question: A boat having a length $3\,m$ and breadth $2\,m$ is floating on a lake. The boat sinks by $1\,cm$ when a man gets on it. The mass of the man is
explanation:
Weight of man = extra upthrust
$\therefore mg = \left( {3 \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right){(10)^3}(10){\text{ }}$
${\text{or }}\quad m = 60\,kg$
subject: Physics

question: A balloon has volume of  $1000\,\,{m^3}$. It is filled with hydrogen $(\rho  = 0.09\,g/L)$. If the density of air is $1.29\,g/L$, it can lift a total weight of
explanation:
$1\,g/L = 1\,kg/{m^3}$
Upthrust $$=$$ total downward force
$\therefore 1000 \times 1.29 \times g = (1000 \times 0.09 \times g + mg)$
$\therefore m = 1200\,kg$
subject: Physics

question: A body floats in a liquid containing in a beaker. The whole system shown in figure is falling under gravity, the upthrust on the body due to the liquid is
explanation: In a freely falling vessel upthrust is zero, because effective value of $$g$$ is zero

options.text: equal to weight of the body in air
equal to weight of the liquid displaced
equal to weight of the immersed part of the body

subject: Physics

question: A body weight 50 grams in air and 40 grams in water. How much would it weigh in a liquid of specific gravity 1.5 ?
subject: Physics

question: Two solids $$P$$ and $$Q$$ float in water. It is observed that $P$ floats with half of its volume immersed and $$Q$$ floats with $$\dfrac{2^{\text {rd }}}{3}$$ of its volume is immersed. The ratio of densities of $P$ and $Q$ is
explanation:
For $$P$$,
Let ${V_P}$ and ${\rho _p}$ be volume and density of solid $P$ respectively.
As the solid $P$ is floating in water,
$$\therefore$$ Weight of $P = $ weight of water displaced by $P$
${V_p}{\rho _p}g = \frac{{{V_P}}}{2}{\rho _{{\rm{water}}}}g$
${\rho _p} = \frac{1}{2}{\rho _{{\rm{water}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( 1 \right)$
Similarly, for solid $Q$
${V_Q}{\rho _Q}g = \frac{2}{3}\,{V_Q}{\rho _{{\text{water }}}}g$
${\rho _Q} = \frac{2}{3}{\rho _{{\rm{water }}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From equation (1) & (2)
$\frac{{{\rho _P}}}{{{\rho _Q}}} = \frac{3}{4}$
subject: Physics

question: An aluminium sphere is dipped into water. Which of the following is true?
explanation: Density of water is maximum at $4^\circ C$, therefore buoyancy at $4^\circ C$ will be maximum

options.text: Buoyancy in water at $0^\circ C$ will be same as that in water at $4^\circ C.$
Buoyancy will be less in water at $0^\circ C$ than that in water at $4^\circ C.$
Buoyancy may be more or less in water at $4^\circ C$ depending on the radius of the sphere
Buoyancy will be more in water at $0^\circ C$ than that in water at $4^\circ C.$

subject: Physics

question: A metal sphere connected by a string is dipped in a liqiud of density $$\rho$$ as shown in figure. The pressure at the bottom of the vessel will be, $\left( {{\rho _o} = } \right.$ atmospheric pressure)
explanation: Upthrust on sphere from the liqiud makes equal and opposite pair of forces. hence there will be no effect on the pressure at bottom of vessel. Or $$\mathrm{p}=\mathrm{p}_{0}+\rho g h$$
subject: Physics

question: Hydraulic lift works on the basis of
explanation: Hydraulic lift is one of the applications of Pascal's law.
subject: Physics
