question: A straw of circular cross-section of radius $R$ and negligible thickness is dipped vertically into a liquid of surface tension $T$. If the contact angle between the liquid and the straw material is $53^{\circ}$. The force acting on the straw due to the surface tension of the liquid is ( $\cos 53^{\circ}=0.6$ )
explanation:
We know that,force due to surface tension is given by
$F=T \times 2($ length $) \times \cos \theta$
$F=T \times 2 l \times \cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow F=T \times 2 \times 2 \pi R \times \cos 53^{\circ}[l=2 \pi R]$
$\Rightarrow F=4 \pi R T \times 0.6 \Rightarrow F=\dfrac{12 \pi R T}{5}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: If the work done in blowing a soap bubble of radius $R$ is $W$, then the work done in blowing the soap bubble of radius $2\,R$ is
explanation:
Given,
Using the formula for work done in blowing soap bubble.
$W=$ Surface Tension $\times$ Change in area.
For initial case ($R$)
${W_1} = W = (2T) \times 4\pi {R^2} \Rightarrow W = 8\pi {R^2}T\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For case ($r=2 R$)
$W_{2}=(2 T) \times 4 \pi(2 R)^{2}$
$W_{2}=32 \pi R^{2} T$
$W_{2}=4\left[8 \pi R^{2} T\right]$
$W_{2}=4 {~W}$
So, the work done will be $4\,W$.
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A vessel having small hole in the bottom has to hold water without leakage, if water is poured into it upto a height of $7\,cm$, then the radius of the hole is (surface tension of water is $0.07 {Nm}^{-1}$, angle of contact is $0^{\circ}$ and $g=10\,m s^{-2}$)
explanation:
Surface tension of water membrane on the water at the hole will be,
$T=\dfrac{F}{2 \pi r}=\dfrac{\rho g h \times \pi r^{2}}{2 \pi r}=\dfrac{\rho g h r}{2}$
$\Rightarrow r=\dfrac{2 T}{\rho g h}$
$=\dfrac{0.07 \times 2}{0.07 \times 1000 \times 10}=2 \times 10^{-4} {~m}=0.2 {~mm}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: The angle of contact is $120^{\circ}$ when a cylindrical rod is vertically placed in a liquid. If the same rod is placed horizontally in the liquid, then the angle of contact is
explanation:
Angle of contact is one of the characteristic of interaction of a liquid with a solid body.
So, it does not depend on inclination or orientation of the rod or capillary tube.
So, angle of contact will be $120^{\circ} {C}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A water drop breaks into 64 identical droplets each of surface area $10^{-7} {~m}^{2}$. If the surface tension of water is $0.07 {Nm}^{-1}$, then the increase in the surface energy in the process is
explanation:
Change in surface energy is
$\Delta(S E)=T \Delta A$
$\Delta (SE) = T\Delta A\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\Delta A=\Delta A_{\text {final }}-\Delta A_{\text {initial }}$
$A_{\text {initial }}=4 \pi r^{2} \quad$ [Area of sphere]
$A_{\text {final }}=4 \pi r_{1}^{2} \times 64 \quad\left[r_{1}=\right.
$ new radius of drops $]$
Volume will remain constant
$V_{\text {initial }}=V_{\text {final }} \Rightarrow \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r_{1}^{3} \times 64$
${r_1} = \frac{r}{4}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$A_{\text {final }}=10^{-7} \times 64=64 \times 10^{-7} {~m}^{2}$
$A_{\text {initial }}=4 \pi\left(4 r_{1}\right)^{2}=16 \times 4 \pi r_{1}^{2}$
$=16 \times 10^{-7} {~m}^{2}$
$\Delta A=\left[\Delta A_{\text {final }}-\Delta A_{\text {initial }}\right]$
$\Delta(S E)=0.07 \times\left[64 \times 10^{-7}-16 \times 10^{-7}\right]$
$\Delta(S E)=336 \times 10^{-9} J$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: An air bubble of radius \[1\,mm\] is at a depth of \[8\,cm\] below the free surface of a liquid column. If the surface tension and density of the liquid is $0.1 {~N} / {m}$ and $2000 {~kg} / {m}^{3}$, respectively, by what amount is the pressure inside the bubble greater than the atmospheric pressure? (take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952652/preptracker/questions/q_109837_explanation_1764952652335.png where, $P_{0}=$ atmospheric pressure,
$R=$ Air bubble of radius $=1 {~mm}, \rho=$ density of liquid $=2000 {~kg} / {m}^{3}$
and $T=$ surface tension of liquid $=0.1 {~N} / {m}$. Now, we know that, excess pressure inside an air bubble inside any liquid $=\dfrac{2 T}{R}$
Also, pressure inside the air bubble $=$ pressure outside + excess pressure
$\Rightarrow P_{\text {inside }}=\underset{\substack{\text { Atmospheric } \\ \text { pressure }}}{P_{0}}+\underset{\substack{\text { Pressure } \\ \text { due to liquid } \\ \text { column }}}{\rho g h}+\underset{\substack{\text { Excess pressure } \\ \text { insidear bubble }}}{\dfrac{2 T}{R}}$
Difference of pressure inside the air bubble and atmospheric pressure,
$\Rightarrow \quad P_{\text {inside }}-P_{0}=\rho g h+\dfrac{2 T}{R}$
$=2000 \times 10 \times \dfrac{8}{100}+\dfrac{2 \times 0.1}{1 \times 10^{-3}} {~N} / {m}^{2}$
$=(1600+200) {N} / {m}^{2}$
$=1800 {~N} / {m}^{2}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A soap bubble of initial radius $R$ is to be blown up. The surface tension of the soap film is $T$. The surface energy needed to double the diameter of the bubble is
explanation:
Work done to increase size of bubble $=$ Change in surface area $\times$ Surface tension https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958155/preptracker/questions/q_109829_explanation_1764958154897.png $\left.4 \pi[2 R)^{2}-R^{2}\right] \times T \times 2=24 \pi R^{2} T$
[Note-Factor 2 appears here because a bubble has two surfaces.]
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A spherical drop of radius $r$ is divided into 8 equal droplets. If the surface tension is $S$, then the work done in the process will be
explanation:
Let $r=$ radius of large drop and $R=$ radius of each of small drops https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952651/preptracker/questions/q_109827_explanation_1764952650938.png As volume remains same,
Initial volume $=$ Final volume
$\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}=8 \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} \Rightarrow r=2 R$ or $R=\dfrac{r}{2}$
Change in surface area due to splitting of drop is
$\Delta A=8 \times 4 \pi R^{2}-4 \pi r^{2}$
$=8 \times 4 \pi\left(\dfrac{r}{2}\right)^{2}-4 \pi r^{2}=4 \pi r^{2}$
$\therefore$ Work done $=S . \Delta A=S .4 \pi r^{2}=4 \pi r^{2} S$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A metal sheet $4\,cm$ on a side and of negligible thickness is attached to a balance and inserted into container fluid. The balance to which metal sheet is attached read $0.50\,N$ and the contact angle is found to be zero. A small amount of oil is then spread over the metal sheet. The contact angle now becomes $180^{\circ}$ and the balance now reads $0.49\,N$. The surface tension of the fluid is
explanation:
As we know that,
Surface tension, $S=\Delta F / 4 l$
where, $\Delta F$ is change in force
$=F_{1}-F_{2}=0.50-0.49=0.01 {~N}$
and $l$ is length of sheet,
$i.e.$ $l=4 {~cm}=4 \times 10^{-2} {~m}$
$\therefore \quad S=\dfrac{\Delta F}{4 l}=\dfrac{0.01}{4 \times 4 \times 10^{-2}}=\dfrac{1}{16}$
$=0.0625 {~N} / {m}$
$=6.25 \times 10^{-2} {~N} / {m}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: Liquid $A$ rises to a height of $10\,cm$ in a capillary tube and liquid $B$ falls to a depth of $2\,cm$ in the same tube. The density of $A$ and $B$ are $1 {~g} / {cm}^{3}$ and $10 {~g} / {cm}^{3}$, respectively. The contact angle of $A$ and $B$ with the tube is $0^{\circ}$ and $135^{\circ}$, respectively. If the surface tension of $A$ and $B$ are $S_{A}$ and $S_{B}$, respectively then the ratio $\dfrac{S_{B}}{S_{A}}$ is
explanation:
As, we know that,
$H($ capillary rise $)=\dfrac{2 S \cos \theta}{\rho g r}$
$\Rightarrow H_{A}=\dfrac{2 S_{A} \cos \theta_{A}}{\rho_{A} g r} \Rightarrow 10=\dfrac{2 S_{A} \cos 0^{\circ}}{1 g r}$
$ \Rightarrow 10gr = 2{S_A}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and $H_{B}=\dfrac{2 S_{B} \cos \theta_{B}}{\rho_{B} g r}$
$\Rightarrow 2 S_{B}=\dfrac{H_{B} \rho_{B} g r}{\cos \theta_{B}}=\dfrac{2 \times 10 g r}{\cos 135^{\circ}}$
$ = - 20\sqrt 2 gr\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
On dividing Eq. (2) by Eq. (1), we get
$\dfrac{S_{B}}{S_{A}}=\dfrac{-20 \sqrt{2} g r}{10 g r}=-2 \sqrt{2}$
$\Rightarrow\left|\dfrac{S_{B}}{S_{A}}\right|=2 \sqrt{2}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: The work done in breaking a drop of liquid of radius $R$ (Surface tension $T$ ) into 64 equal drops is
explanation:
In the process of breaking the drop, the volume of liquid drops remains constant.
$V_{i}=V_{f}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=n\left(\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}\right)$
$R^{3}=64 r^{3} \Rightarrow R=4 r \Rightarrow r=\dfrac{R}{4}$
Now, work done in breaking a big drop in 64 small drops.
$W=$ final energy $-$ initial energy
$W=T\left(n 4 \pi r^{2}\right)-T\left(4 \pi R^{2}\right)$
$=4 \pi T\left[64\left(\dfrac{R}{4}\right)^{2}-R^{2}\right]$
$=4 \pi T\left[4 R^{2}-R^{2}\right]=12 \pi R^{2} T$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A mercury drop of radius $1\,cm$ is sprayed into $10^{6}$ droplets of equal size. Calculate the energy expended if surface tension of mercury is $435 \times 10^{-3} {~N} / {m}$.
explanation:
Work done in splitting a large drop of radius $R$ in $n$ smaller drops is, $W=T \times \Delta A$
Where $T$ is Surface tension and $\Delta A$ is change in surface area.
Now, $n \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}$
$\Rightarrow r=R / n^{1 / 3}$
$\therefore \Delta A=4 \pi\left(n r^{2}-R^{2}\right)=4 \pi R^{2}\left(n^{1 / 3}-1\right)$
$\therefore W=\left(4 \pi R^{2}\right) T \cdot\left(n^{1 / 3}-1\right)$
$ = \left( \begin{gathered} 4 \times \pi \times {\left( {1 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)^2} \times \hfill \\ 435 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times \left( {{{\left( {{{10}^6}} \right)}^{1/3}} - 1} \right) \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right)$
$=54.1 \times 10^{-3} {~J}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: The change in surface energy when a big spherical drop fo radius $R$ is split into $n$ spherical droplets of radius $r$ is ($T=$ surface tension)
explanation:
According to given situation,
Volume of big drop $=$ Volume of $n$ small drops
$\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=n \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \Rightarrow r^{3}=\dfrac{R^{3}}{n}$ or $r=\dfrac{R}{n^{1 / 3}}$
$n{r^2} = \frac{{n{R^2}}}{{{n^{2/3}}}} = {n^{1/3}}{R^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Surface area of big drop, $A=4 \pi R^{2}$
Surface area of $n$ small drops,
$A^{\prime}=n \times 4 \pi r^{2}$
$\Delta A=A^{\prime}-A=4 \pi n r^{2}-4 \pi R^{2}=4 \pi\left(n r^{2}-R^{2}\right)$
$=4 \pi\left(n^{1 / 3} R^{2}-R^{2}\right) \quad$ [from Eq.(1)]
$=4 \pi R^{2}\left(n^{1 / 3}-1\right)$
The work done for change in area,
$W=T \times \Delta A=T \times 4 \pi R^{2}\left(n^{1 / 3}-1\right)$
This work is stored in the form of surface energy. So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: The surface tension of 70 dynes $/ {cm}$ is equal to
explanation:
Surface tension
$=70 \dfrac{\text { dynes }}{{cm}}=70 \times \dfrac{10^{-5}}{10^{-2}} {~N} / {m}$
$=70 \times 10^{-3} {~N} / {m}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: The surface tension of the soap water solution is $\dfrac{1}{10 \pi} {~N} / {m}$. The free energy of the surface layer of a soap bubble of diameter $5\,mm$ will be
explanation:
As, we know that the surface energy of a soap bubble,
$U=2 A T=2 \times 4 \pi\left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{2} T$
$\therefore U=2\left(\pi D^{2}\right) T=2 \times \pi \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{1}{10 \pi}$
$\Rightarrow U=5 \times 10^{-6} {~J}$
So, the option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: Consider a water droplet of diameter $0.2\,mm$ where the outside pressure is $1.5 {~N} / {cm}^{2}$ at $25^{\circ} {C}$. The absolute pressure inside the droplet, when the surface tension at $25^{\circ} {C}$ is $0.08 {~N} / {m}$ is
explanation:
Pressure inside droplet, $P=\dfrac{2 T}{r}+P_{0}$
Putting the given values, we get
$=\dfrac{2 \times 0.08}{10^{-4}}+1.5 \times 10^{4} {~N} / {m}^{2}$
$=0.16 \times 10^{4}+1.5 \times 10^{4}=1.66 \times 10^{4} {~N} / {m}^{2}$
$=1.66 {~N} / {cm}^{2}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A hydrophilic surface is characterised by the contact angle at the water solid interface. The value of contact angle should be
explanation:
For a hydrophilic surface, the contact angle at the water solid interface is less than $90^{\circ}$ $i.e.,$ $ < 90^{\circ}$.
Since, adhesive force between liquid and hydrophilic surface is very high. Hence, the value of contact angle should be less than $90^{\circ}$.
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: Consider an air bubble of radius $2\,mm$ in a liquid at a depth of $5\,cm$ below the free surface. The density of the liquid is $1000 {~kg} / {m}^{3}$ and the surface tension is $0.1 {~N} / {m}$. Then, find the total excess pressure inside the air bubble relative to the pressure at the free surface of the liquid. (Take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
Gauge pressure due to liquid at a depth of $5\,cm$ is given by,
$P=\rho g h$
$\Rightarrow P=1000 \times 10 \times 0.05 \Rightarrow P=500 {~Pa}$
$\Rightarrow$ Excess pressure inside the air bubble w.r.t. outside due to surface tension is $P^{\prime}=\dfrac{2 S}{R}$
Surface tension, $S=0.1 {~N} / {m}$
Radius, $R=2 {~mm}=0.002 {~m}$
$\Rightarrow P^{\prime}=\dfrac{2 \times 0.1}{0.002}=100 {~Pa}$
Now, total excess pressure inside the bubble w.r.t surface of liquid, $i.e.$ $\Rightarrow \quad P_{\text {total }}=P+P^{\prime}=500+100=600 {~Pa}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: Consider a water tank shown in the figure. It has one wall at $x=L$ and can be taken to be very wide in the $z$ direction. When filled with a liquid of surface tension $S$ and density $\rho$, the liquid surface makes angle $\theta_{0}\left(\theta_{0} \ll 1\right)$ with the $x$-axis at $x=L$. If $y(x)$ is the height of the surface then the equation for $y(x)$ is:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952636/preptracker/questions/q_109793_explanation_1764952636034.png $R=$ Radius of curvature at point $C$
Curvature $=\dfrac{1}{R}=\dfrac{\left|\dfrac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right|}{\left(1+\left(\dfrac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}$
$\dfrac{1}{R}=\dfrac{\left|\dfrac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right|}{(1+0)^{\frac{3}{2}}}=\dfrac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} \quad\left[\begin{array}{l}\text { Since } \theta_{0} \ll 1 \\ \text { so } \dfrac{d y}{d x}=\tan \theta \simeq 0\end{array}\right]$
As $A$ and $B$ are at same horizontal line.
$P_{A}=P_{B}=P_{0} \Rightarrow P_{C}=P_{0}-\rho g y$
$P_{C}=P_{0}-\dfrac{S}{R}$
$\Rightarrow \rho g y=S \dfrac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\dfrac{\rho g y}{s}$
subject: Physics

question: A balloon is made of a material of surface tension $S$ and its inflation outlet (from where gas is filled in it) has small area $A$. It is filled with a gas of density $\rho$ and takes a spherical shape of radius $R$. When the gas is allowed to flow freely out of it, its radius $r$ changes from $R$ to 0 (zero) in time $T$. If the speed $v(r)$ of gas coming out of the balloon depends on $r$ as $r^{a}$ and $T \propto S^{\alpha} A^{\beta} \rho^{\gamma} R^{\delta}$ then
explanation:
From the given information we should assume that the given material is not balloon, but it must be a bubble (since balloon does not have surface tension instead it has elasticity). Assume that the balloon radius is r at orbitrary time $t$. Let $v$ be the velocity of the air that comes out from the bubble. Excess pressure inside the bubble is given by
$\Delta P = \frac{{4S}}{r}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958139/preptracker/questions/q_109792_explanation_1764958139482.png From Bernoulli's principle
$P_{1}+\dfrac{1}{2} \rho(0)^{2}+\rho g h=P_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{2}+\rho g h$$P_{1}-P_{2}=\Delta P=\dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{2}=\dfrac{4 S}{r}$
$ \Rightarrow v = 2\sqrt 2 {S^{1/2}}{r^{ - 1/2}}{\rho ^{ - 1/2}}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Given that $v \propto r^{-1 / 2} \Rightarrow a=\dfrac{-1}{2}$
Now at time $t$ volume of the bubble is
$v = \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3} \Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 4\pi {r^2}\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From equation of continuity $\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = - vA\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
From eq's 1, 2 & 3
$4\pi {r^2}\frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = - 2\sqrt 2 {S^{1/2}}{r^{1/2}}{\rho ^{ - 1/2}}A$
${r^{5/2}}\int_R^0 d r = - \frac{{{S^{1/2}}{\rho ^{ - 1/2}}A}}{{\sqrt 2 \pi }}\int_0^T d t$
$\left[ {{r^{7/2}}} \right]_R^0 = - \frac{{{S^{1/2}}{\rho ^{ - 1/2}}AT}}{{\sqrt 2 \pi }}$
$ \Rightarrow T = \sqrt 2 \pi {A^{1/2}}{S^{ - 1/2}}{\rho ^{1/2}}{r^{7/2}}$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = - \frac{1}{2},\beta = - 1,\gamma = \frac{1}{2},\gamma = \frac{7}{2}$.
So the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: Find the radius of curvature of the common surface of two bubbles $\left(R_{1}>R_{2}\right)$
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952631/preptracker/questions/q_109789_explanation_1764952631283.png Excess pressure in a bubble of liquid having radius $R$ and surface tension $T$ is
$P-P_{0}=\dfrac{4 T}{R}$ where $P_{0}$ is the external pressure
For Bubble 1: $P_{1}-P_{0}=\dfrac{4 T}{R_{1}}$
For Bubble 2: $P_{2}-P_{0}=\dfrac{4 T}{R_{2}}$
As, $R_{1}>R_{2}, P_{2}>P_{1}$
Let radius of curvature of common surface between the two bubbles be $R$. Then :
$P_{2}-P_{1}=\dfrac{4 T}{R}$
$\Rightarrow\left(P_{2}-P_{0}\right)-\left(P_{1}-P_{0}\right)=\dfrac{4 T}{R}$
$\therefore \dfrac{4 T}{R_{2}}-\dfrac{4 T}{R_{1}}=\dfrac{4 T}{R} \Rightarrow R=\dfrac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1}-R_{2}}$
So, correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question:
Hot water is less viscous than cold water.
Surface tension of soap bubble is more than that of a drop of water.
explanation: Statement A is true because, due to increase in temperature, viscosity of liquid decreases. Statement B is false because, due to partially soluble impurity such as soap in water, surface tension of water decreases. So, correct option is (2).

options.text: Statement A is true and statement B is true
Statement A is true and statement B is false
Statement A is false and statement B is true
Statement A is false and statement B is false

subject: Physics

question: A small liquid drop of radius $R$ is divided into 27 identical liquid drops. If the surface tension is $T$, then the work done in the process will be
explanation:
Radius of big drop $=R$
Number of drops $n=27$
Surface tension $=T$
$W=4 \pi R^{2} T\left(n^{\frac{1}{3}}-1\right)$
$W=4 \pi R^{2} T\left(27^{\frac{1}{3}}-1\right)$
$=8 \pi R^{2} T$
subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below.
If a capillary tube is immersed first in cold water and then in hot water, the height of capillary rise will be smaller in hot water.
If a capillary tube is immersed first in cold water and then in hot water, the height of capillary rise will be smaller in cold water.
explanation:
The height upto which a liquid rises in capillary tube is given by,
$h=\dfrac{2 T \cos \theta}{r \rho g} \Rightarrow h \propto \dfrac{T}{r}$
Surface tension of hot water is less than that of cold water. When a capillary tube is immersed in hot water, it expands and its radius increases.
As surface tension decreases and radius increases, the height of capillary rise will be smaller in hot water than in cold water.
Hence, statement A is true and statement B is false. So, correct option is (1).

options.text: Statement A is correct but statement B is incorrect
Statement A is incorrect but statement B is correct

subject: Physics

question: A big drop is formed by coalescing 1000 small droplets of water. The surface energy will become
explanation:
Let radius of big drop is $R$ and radius of small drop is $r$. Here, volume remain constant.
Final volume $=$ Initial volume
So, $\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=1000 \cdot \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}$
$R^{3}=10^{3} r^{3} \Rightarrow R=10 r$
Initial surface energy, $U_{i}=1000 \times T \times$ Area
$=1000 \times T \times 4 \pi r^{2}$
Final surface energy,
$U_{f}=T \times 4 \pi R^{2}=T \times 4 \pi(10 r)^{2}$
$=100 \times T \times 4 \pi r^{2}$
$\dfrac{U_{f}}{U_{i}}=\dfrac{100 \times 4 \pi r^{2}}{1000 \times 4 \pi r^{2}} \Rightarrow U_{f}=\dfrac{1}{10} U_{i}$
Thus, surface energy will become $1 / 10^{\text {th }}$ times.
subject: Physics

question: The excess pressure inside a soap bubble is thrice the excess pressure inside a second soap bubble. The ratio between the volume of the first and the second bubble is
explanation:
$\Delta P_{1}=P_{1}-P_{0}=\dfrac{4 T}{r_{1}}$ $\quad \quad (1)$
$\Delta P_{2}=P_{2}-P_{0}=\dfrac{4 T}{r_{2}}$ $\quad \quad (2)$
According to question, $\Delta P_{1}=3 \Delta P_{2}$
$\therefore \dfrac{4 T}{r_{1}}=3 \times \dfrac{4 T}{r_{2}} \Rightarrow r_{2}=3 r_{1}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r_{1}^{3}}{\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r_{2}^{3}}=\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)^{3}=\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{3 r_{1}}\right)^{3}=1: 27$
subject: Physics

question: Pressure inside a soap bubble is greater than the pressure outside by an amount (given : $R=$ Radius of bubble, $S=$ Surface tension of bubble).
explanation:
Inside a soap bubble, the excess pressure is given by
$\Delta P=\dfrac{4 S}{R} \quad$ (as it has two free surfaces)
Where $S=$ Surface tension
$R=$ Radius of soap bubble
subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below.
When a capillary tube is dipped into a liquid, the liquid neither rises nor falls in the capillary. The contact angle may be $0^{\circ}$.
The contact angle between a solid and a liquid is a property of the material of the solid and liquid as well.
explanation:
Rise or fall of capillary height,
$h=\dfrac{2 T}{\rho g r} \cos \theta$
If $\theta < 90^{\circ}, h$ is $+v e \Rightarrow$ rise of liquid
If $\theta>90^{\circ}, h$ is $-v e \Rightarrow$ fall of liquid
For $h$ be zero, contact angle $\theta=90^{\circ}$.
(Statement A is incorrect.)
Contact angle between a solid and liquid depends upon the adhesive and cohesive forces between the two media. These forces are the properties of the material in contact $i.\,e.,$ solid and liquid, which decides the contact angle.
(Statement B is correct.)
So, option (2) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect and statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below.
The contact angle between a solid and a liquid is a property of the material of the solid and liquid as well.
The rise of a liquid in a capillary tube does not depend on the inner radius of the tube.
explanation:
Statement A is correct as the contact angle depends on the interaction between solid and liquid.
From the ascent formula, rise in capillary,
$h=\dfrac{2 T}{\rho g r} \cos \theta$
Thus, rise of a liquid in a capillary tube depends on surface tension $(T)$, density of liquid $(\rho)$, contact angle $(\theta)$ and inner radius of capillary $(r)$.

options.text: Statement A is correct but statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A bowl whose bottom has round holes of diameter $1\,mm$ is filled with water. Assuming that surface tension acts only at holes, find the maximum height  up to which water can be filled in the vessel without leakage. (Given, surface tension of water ${=75 \times 10^{-3} {Nm}^{-1}, g=10 {~ms}^{-2}}$ and density of water ${=1 {~g} / {cm}^{3}}$.
explanation:
The vertical force due to surface at the hole https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956068/preptracker/questions/q_39138_explanation_1764956068340.png $=T \cos \theta \times L=T \cos \theta \times 2 \pi r$
This will balance weight.
${m g\left(=\pi r^{2} h \rho g\right)}$
${\therefore \quad T \cos \theta \times 2 \pi r=\pi r^{2} h \rho g}$
$\Rightarrow \quad h=\dfrac{2 T \cos \theta}{r \rho g}$
${h}$ is maximum, when ${\cos \theta=1}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {h_{\max }} = \frac{{2T}}{{r\rho g}}$
$ = \frac{{2 \times 0.075}}{{0.0005 \times 1000 \times 10}} = 0.03\,m = 3\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A long capillary tube of radius $0.2\,mm$ is placed vertically inside a beaker of water of surface tension $0.07\,N{\rm{/}}m.$ Water due to zero angle of contact rises upto height of $7.05\,cm$ in tube. Now if tube is pushed into water so that only $5\,cm$ of its length is above the surface, find new angle of contact in degrees between water and glass surface. (Take $\sqrt{2}=1.41$ )
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957022/preptracker/questions/q_39102_explanation_1764957022033.png If two points are just above and below the meniscus then, $\rho g{h_0} = \frac{{2\,T}}{R}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
When $\theta = 0;R = r$ and ${h_0} = h$
$\therefore \rho gh = \frac{{2\,T}}{r}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From equations (1) and (2),
$ \Rightarrow \frac{r}{R} = \frac{{{h_0}}}{{\,h}}$
$\therefore $ New angle of contact,
$\theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{r}{R}} \right) = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{{h_0}}}{{\,h}}} \right)$
$ = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{5}{{7.05}}} \right)$
$ = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{5}{{5 \times \sqrt 2 }}} \right) = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)$
$\therefore \theta = 45^\circ $
subject: Physics

question: A drop of some liquid of volume $0.09\,c{m^3}$ is placed on the surface of a glass slide. Then another glass slide is placed on it in such a way that the liquid forms a thin layer of area $30\,c{m^2}$ between the surfaces of the two slides. To separate the slides, a force of $16 \times 10^{5}$ dyne has to be applied normal to the surfaces. The surface tension of the liquid (in $dyne - c{m^{ - 1}}$) is
explanation:
Let thickness of layer be $t$
$V = At \Rightarrow t = \frac{V}{A}$
$2r = \frac{V}{A} \Rightarrow r = \frac{V}{{2\,A}}$
also, $\Delta P = \frac{T}{r}$
$\therefore F = \Delta P \times A = \frac{T}{r} \times A = \frac{T}{{\left( {\frac{V}{{2\,A}}} \right)}}A = \frac{{2T{A^2}}}{{\,V}}$
$\therefore \quad T = \frac{{FV}}{{2\,{A^2}}} = \frac{{16 \times {{10}^5} \times 0.09}}{{2 \times {{(30)}^2}}} = 80\,dyne/cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Two Parallel Plates having Liquid between Them

question: A metal wire of density $\rho$ floats on water surface horizontally. For it not to sink in water maximum possible radius of wire should be $\frac{{{T^n}}}{{\sqrt {\pi \rho g} }}$ where, $T$ is surface tension of water. Find $n$.
explanation:
For wire to float into water, its weight should be balanced by the surface tension of the water.
$\therefore \,\,mg = Tl\quad ({\rm{where}},\,\,l = {\rm{length}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{the}}\,\,{\rm{wire}})$
$\therefore \quad V\rho g = Tl$
$\therefore \pi {r^2}l\rho g = Tl$
$\therefore \quad {r^2} = \frac{T}{{\pi \rho g}}$
$\therefore \quad r = \sqrt {\frac{T}{{\pi \rho g}}} \Rightarrow {T^n} = \sqrt T $
$\therefore \quad n = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$
subject: Physics

question: A bubble having surface tension $T$ and radius $R$ is formed on ring of radius $r(r \ll R)$. Air of density $\rho$ is blown inside the tube with velocity $v$ such that air molecule collides perpendicularly with the wall of the bubble and stops. The radius at which the bubble separates from the ring is $\frac{{ZT}}{{\rho {v^2}}}.$ Find $Z$.
explanation:
Excess pressure inside a bubble $ = \frac{{4T}}{R}$ Let area of wall of bubble where air strikes be $A$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956065/preptracker/questions/q_39053_explanation_1764956065087.png $\therefore \quad$ Force due to excess pressure $ = \frac{{4TA}}{R}$
Now, force due to striking air $ = \rho A{v^2}$
For bubble to separate from the ring,
$\rho A v^{2}=\dfrac{4 T A}{R} \Rightarrow R=\dfrac{4 T}{\rho v^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow Z=4$
subject: Physics

question: Maximum possible mass of a greased needle of length $l$ floating on water surface of tension $T$ is $CTl.$ Find value of $C.$ (Take $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$)
explanation:
Let the mass of the needle be $m$. As the liquid surface is distorted, the surface tension forces acing on both sides of the needle make an angle $\theta$, say, with vertical as shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956902/preptracker/questions/q_39041_explanation_1764956901662.png Since the forces acting on the needle are $F,F$ and $mg$, resolving the forces vertically for its equilibrium,
$\sum {{F_y}} = F\cos \theta + F\cos \theta - mg = 0$
$ \Rightarrow m = \frac{{2\,F\cos \theta }}{g}$ where $F = Tl$
$\therefore m = \frac{{2\,Tl\cos \theta }}{g}$
For $m$ to be maximum, $\cos \theta=1$
$\therefore {m_{\max }} = \frac{{2\,Tl}}{{\,g}} = \frac{{2Tl}}{{10}} = 0.2\,Tl$
$ \Rightarrow C = 0.2$
subject: Physics

question: A capillary tube of radius $r$ is immersed in water and water rises in it to a height $h$. The mass of water in the capillary tube is $12\,g.$  If another capillary of radius $4 r$ is immersed in water, then the mass of water that would rise in this tube will be
explanation:
Let ${h_1}$ be the capillary rise in tube of radius $r$.
$\therefore {h_1} = \frac{{2\,T\cos \theta }}{{r\rho g}}$
Mass of water in the first tube is
${m_1} = \pi {r^2}{h_1}\rho = \pi {r^2}\left( {\frac{{2T\cos \theta }}{{r\rho g}}} \right)\rho $
$ = \frac{{2\pi rT\cos \theta }}{g}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {m_1} \propto r$
$\therefore \quad \frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}} = \frac{{{r_2}}}{{{r_1}}} = \frac{{4r}}{r} = 4$
$\therefore \quad {m_2} = 4{m_1}$
$\therefore \quad {m_2} = 4 \times 12 = 48\,g$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform capillary tube of length $l$ and inner radius $r$ with its upper end sealed is submerged vertically into water. The outside pressure is ${P_0}$ and surface tension of water is $T$. When water levels inside and outside capillary coincide, length of capillary submerged into water is $\dfrac{2 l}{\left(2+\dfrac{P_{0} r}{c T}\right)}$. Find value of $c$.
explanation:
Let $x$ be the length of capillary submerged. In equilibrium for air inside capillary,
${P_0}(l\,A) = P'(l - x)A$
Where, $P'$ is pressure in capillary after being submerged into water.
$\therefore \quad P' = \frac{{{P_0}l}}{{l - x}}$
Since level of water inside capillary coincides with outside, the excess pressure,
$\Delta P = P' - {P_0} = \frac{{2\,T}}{r}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{{{P_0}l}}{{l - x}} - {P_0} = \frac{{2\,T}}{r}$
Solving above equation,
$x = \frac{l}{{\left( {1 + \frac{{{{\mathop P\limits^. }_0}r}}{{2\,T}}} \right)}}$
Comparing with given relation,
$\frac{{2l}}{{2 + \frac{{{P_0}r}}{{cT}}}} = \frac{l}{{\left( {1 + \frac{{{P_0}r}}{{2\,T}}} \right)}}$
$ \Rightarrow c = 1$
subject: Physics

question:
There is a small hole in a hollow sphere. When it is taken to a depth of $20\,cm$ under water, water enters in it. If the surface tension of water is $0.07\,N{\rm{/}}m,$ the radius of the hole in $\mu m$ is
(Take $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$)
explanation:
When water enters the hollow sphere, force of surface tension is balanced by normal force exerted by the liquid column,
$i.e.,{\rm{ }}T \times 2\pi r = \pi {r^2}h\rho g$
where, $r$ is radius of hole of the sphere.
$\therefore \quad r = \frac{{2\,T}}{{\,h\rho g}} = \frac{{2 \times 0.07}}{{20 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times {{10}^3} \times 10}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 70 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,m$
$\therefore \quad r = 70\mu m$
subject: Physics

question: A rectangular frame of $7\,cm \times 5\,cm$ hangs vertically by an arm of a balance. When its edge is just dipped in soap solution in container, extra mass of $0.4\,g$ must be placed in opposite arm to balance the pull of the film in order to just raise it. Determine surface tension of soap solution in ${\rm{dyne/}}cm$.
explanation:
A soap film is formed between edge length of frame and soap solution in container.
$\therefore \quad T = \frac{{mg}}{{2l}} = \frac{{0.4 \times 980}}{{2 \times 7}} = 28\,dyne/cm$
subject: Physics

question: If the temperature increases, the surface tension of a liquid
explanation:
The surface tension of a liquid decreases with the rise in temperature and it disappears a few degrees below the critical temperature.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: Excess pressure inside a soap bubble is three times that of the other bubble. The ratio of their volumes will be
explanation:
In first bubble, excess pressure ${\left(P_{1}\right)=\dfrac{4 T}{R_{1}}}$
In second bubble, excess pressure ${\left(P_{2}\right)=\dfrac{4 T}{R_{2}}}$Given ${P_{1}=3 P_{2}}$
${\therefore \dfrac{4 T}{R_{1}}=3 \times \dfrac{4 T}{R_{2}}}$ or ${\dfrac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{3}}$
${\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}\right)^{3}=\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^{3}=\dfrac{1}{27}}$.
So correct option is (4)
subject: Physics

question: Due to capillary action, a liquid will rise in a tube if angle of contact is
explanation: $h = \frac{{2\,T\cos \theta }}{{r\rho g}}$; The liquid will rise $i.e.,$ $h$ is positive if $\cos \theta$ is $ + ve;$ It is so if $\theta < 90^{\circ}$ or $\theta$ is acute.
subject: Physics

question: Two circular plates of radius $5\,cm$ each, have a $0.01\,mm$ thick water film between them. Then what will be the force required to separate these plate (S.T. of water $ = 73\,dyne{\rm{/}}cm$)?
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Two Parallel Plates having Liquid between Them

question: Two mercury drops (each of radius $r$) merge to form a bigger drop. The surface energy of the bigger drop, if $T$ is the surface tension, is
explanation:
$\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=2 \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=2^{1 / 3} r$
Surface energy of bigger drop,
$E = 4\,\pi {R^2}T = 4 \times {2^{2/3}}\pi {r^2}T = {2^{8/3}}\pi {r^2}T$
subject: Physics

question: If the length of tube is less and cannot accommodate the maximum rise of liquid, then
explanation: If the length of the tube $h^{\prime}$ is less than $h$, it is found that the liquid does not overflow. In a tube of insufficient length, the liquid rises upto the top of the tube and increases the radius of curvature of its meniscus to a value $R^{\prime}$, i.e. smaller the length $h^{\prime}$ of the tube, greater will be the radius of curvature $R^{\prime}$ of the meniscus, but the liquid will never overflow.

options.text: the meniscus will adjust itself, so that the water does not spill

subject: Physics

question: Find the difference of air pressure between the inside and outside of a soap bubble $5\,mm$ in diameter, if the surface tension is $1.6\,\,N{m^{ - 1}}$.
explanation:
The excess pressure $p$ of bubble in air is given by,
$p = \frac{{4T}}{R} = \frac{{4 \times 1.6}}{{2.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 2560\,\,N{m^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics

question: Two soap bubbles have radii in the ratio of $2: 1$. What is the ratio of excess pressures inside them?
explanation:
Excess pressure inside the bubble is given by, $p=4 T / r$
Then,
${p_1} = 4T/{r_1}\quad \quad \quad \,(1)$
${p_2} = 4T/{r_2}\quad \quad \quad (2)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we have
$\dfrac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}=\dfrac{4 T / r_{1}}{4 T / r_{2}}=\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A bubble of  $8\,mm$  diameter is formed in the air. The surface tension of soap solution is $30\,{\mkern 1mu} dyne/cm.$ The excess pressure inside the bubble is
explanation:
The excess pressure inside the bubble,
$\Delta \,p = \frac{{4T}}{r} = \frac{{4 \times 30}}{{0.4}} = 300{\rm{ }}dyne/c{m^2}$
subject: Physics

question: A liquid drop of diameter $D$ breaks into 27 small drops of equal size. If the surface tension of the liquid is $\sigma$, then change in surface energy is
explanation:
Work done, $W=T \times \Delta A$
We know that, work done in splitting $n$ liquid drops,
$W=4 \pi \sigma R^{2}\left[n^{1 / 3}-1\right]$
$=4 \pi \sigma\left[\dfrac{D}{2}\right]^{2}\left[(27)^{1 / 3}-1\right]$
$=\pi D^{2} \sigma\left[(27)^{1 / 3}-1\right]=2 \pi D^{2} \sigma$
subject: Physics

question: Two soap bubbles coalesce. It is noticed that, whilst Joined together, the radii of the two bubbles are $a$ and $b$ where $a>b$. Then, the radius of curvature of interface between the two bubbles will be
explanation:
According to given figure,Let the radius of curvature of the common internal film surface of the https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956687/preptracker/questions/q_38853_explanation_1764956687280.png double bubble formed by two bubbles be $r$. Excess of pressure as compared to atmosphere inside $A$ is $(4 T / a)$. In double bubble, the pressure difference between $A$ and $B$ on either side of the common surface
$=\dfrac{4 T}{b}-\dfrac{4 T}{a}$
This will be equal to $(4 T / r)$.
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad \dfrac{4 T}{r}=\dfrac{4 T}{b}-\dfrac{4 T}{a} \\& \text { or } \dfrac{1}{r}=\left(\dfrac{1}{b}-\dfrac{1}{a}\right)=\dfrac{a-b}{b a} \\& \text { or } \quad r=\dfrac{a b}{a-b}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The work done in blowing a soap bubble of surface tension  $0.06\,\,N{m^{ - 1}}$ from $2\,cm$ radius to $5\,cm$ radius is
explanation:
Here, $S = 0.06\,\,N{m^{ - 1}}$
${r_1} = 2\,cm = 0.02\,m,\,\,{r_2} = 5\,cm = 0.05\,m$
Since, bubble has two surfaces.
Initial surface area of the bubble
$=2 \times 4 \pi r_{1}^{2}=2 \times 4 \pi(0.02)^{2}$
$ = 32\pi \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{m^2}$
Final surface area of the bubble
$=2 \times 4 \pi r_{2}^{2}=2 \times 4 \pi(0.05)^{2}$
$ = 200\pi \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{m^2}$
Increase in surface area
$=200 \pi \times 10^{-4}-32 \pi \times 10^{-4}$
$ = 168\,\pi \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{m^2}$
$\therefore$ Work done $=$ Surface tension $(S)$ $\times$ Increase in surface area
$0.06 \times 168\,\,\pi \times {10^{ - 4}} = 0.003168\,J$
subject: Physics

question: The volume of a spherical soap bubble ${A}$ is 8 times the volume of the soap bubble ${B}$. The ratio of the excess pressure inside the two soap bubbles will be
explanation:
${\dfrac{R_{A}}{R_{B}}=\dfrac{2}{1}}$
Since ${P \propto \dfrac{1}{r}}$
${\dfrac{P_{A}}{P_{B}}=\dfrac{r_{B}}{r_{A}}=\dfrac{1}{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: If million small drops of water coalesce into one larger drop, then the ratio of total surface energy of the larger drop to that of the smaller drops combined will be
explanation:
If radius of small drop is ${r}$, then radius of larger drop, ${R}$ is ${\left(10^{6}\right)^{1 / 3}}$.
$\Rightarrow R=100 r$
$\therefore $ ratio is $1:{10^2}$
subject: Physics

question: A thin flat circular disc of radius 4.5 cm is placed gently over the surface of water. If surface tension of water is ${0.07 {Nm}^{-1}}$, then the excess force required to take it away from the surface is
explanation:
Surface tension force acts along the circumference of the disc. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956660/preptracker/questions/q_38747_explanation_1764956659748.png Excess force required to balance the surface tension force is ${F_{ext}} = T \times 2\pi R$
${F_{ext}} = \frac{7}{{100}} \times 2 \times 3.14 \times \frac{{4.5}}{{100}}$
$ = 197.82 \times {10^{ - 4}} = 19.8\,mN$
subject: Physics

question: If 1000 droplets of water of surface tension $0.07\,N/m$, having same radius $1\,mm$ each, combine to form a single drop. In the process the released surface energy is $\left( {{\rm{Take }}\,\pi  = \frac{{22}}{7}} \right)$
explanation:
Volume of a bigger single drop $=1000 \times$ volume of a smaller drop
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=1000 \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}$
$\Rightarrow R^{3}=1000 r^{3} \Rightarrow R=10 r$
Decrease in the surface area,
$\Delta A=1000 \times 4 \pi r^{2}-4 \pi R^{2}$
$=1000 \times 4 \pi r^{2}-4 \pi(10 r)^{2}=900 \times 4 \pi r^{2}$
Here $r = 1\,mm = {10^{ - 3}}\,m$,
$S = 0.07\,N/m$
$\Delta E=$?
Required energy loss in the process,
$\Delta E=$ Surface tension $\times$ change in surface area
$\Delta E=S . \Delta A$
$\Delta E=0.07 \times 900 \times 4 \times(22 / 7) \times\left(10^{-3}\right)^{2}$
$\Delta E = 7.92 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,J$
subject: Physics

question: A mercury drop of radius ${10^{ - 3}}\,m$ is broken into 125 equal size droplets. Surface tension of mercury is $0.45\,N{m^{ - 1}}$. The gain in surface energy is
explanation:
Radius of bigger drop, $R = {10^{ - 3}}\,m$
Number of drops, $n=125$
Surface tension, $T = 0.45\,N/m$
Let the radius of smaller drop is $r$.
Volume of bigger drop $=$ Sum of volume of smaller drops
So, $\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=n \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \Rightarrow\left(10^{-3}\right)^{3}=125 r^{3}$
${\left( {\frac{{{{10}^{ - 3}}}}{5}} \right)^3} = {r^3} \Rightarrow r = \frac{1}{5} \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m$
Initial surface energy, $U_{i}=T \times 4 \pi R^{2}$
Final surface energy, $U_{f}=n \times T \times 4 \pi r^{2}$
$\Delta U=U_{f}-U_{i}=T \times 4 \pi\left[n r^{2}-R^{2}\right]$
$=0.45 \times 4 \times 3.14\left[125 \times \dfrac{10^{-6}}{25}-10^{-6}\right]$
$ = 2.26 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,J$
So, correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: The height of liquid column raised in a capillary tube of certain radius when dipped in liquid \(A\) vertically is, \[5\;cm\]. If the tube is dipped in a similar manner in another liquid \(B\) of surface tension and density double the values of liquid \(A\), the height of liquid column raised in liquid \(B\) would be ____ \(m\).
explanation:
$h=\dfrac{2 T \cos \theta}{R \rho g}$
$\therefore h \propto \dfrac{T}{P}$
So, on increasing the surface tension $T$ and density $\rho$ by same factor 2 (by introducing another liquid) there is no change in height of liquid raised. So, correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question:
Surface tension of a soap bubble is $2.0 \times {10^{ - 2}}N{m^{ - 1}}$. Work done to increase the radius of soap bubble from $3.5\,cm$ to $7\,cm$ will be
Take $\left[\pi=\dfrac{22}{7}\right]$
explanation:
Surface tension of a soap bubble,
$T = 2 \times {10^{ - 2}}N{m^{ - 1}}$
Work done to increase the radius of soap bubble from $3.5\,cm$ to $7\,cm$.
$W=$ Change in surface energy
$=$ Surface Tension $\left(A_{f}-A_{i}\right)$
$W=2 T(\Delta A)$
$W=2 T\left(4 \pi R_{2}^{2}-4 \pi R_{1}^{2}\right)$
$W=2 \times 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 4 \pi\left[\begin{array}{c}\left(7 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2} \\ -\left(3.5 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}\end{array}\right]$
$ \Rightarrow W \approx 18.48 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,J$
subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Viscosity of gases is greater than that of liquids.
Surface tension of a liquid decreases due to the presence of insoluble impurities.
explanation: Viscosity represent resistance of flow & in liquids molecules are closely packed. So viscosity of liquids is higher than gases. Surface tension of liquid increases on adding soluble material as they make bond with solvent increasing density & thus increasing intermolecular attractions. But on adding insoluble impurities, surface tension of liquid decreases. So option (2) is right.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A needle placed carefully on the surface of water may float, whereas the ball of the same material will always sink.

Reason: The buoyancy of an object does not depend on both on the materials and shape of the object.
explanation: When a needle is gently placed on the surface of water, it remains floating due to the surface tension of water, which prevents the needle from sinking. However, in the case of a steel ball, the surface tension of the water is insufficient to keep it afloat, causing it to sink. Buoyancy force depends on density and so on material of object. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: When the height of a tube is less than the liquid rise in the capillary tube, the liquid does not overflow.

Reason: The product of the radius of the meniscus and the height of the liquid in the capillary tube always remains constant.
explanation: When the value of $h$ decreases due to an insufficient tube length, the radius of curvature of the liquid meniscus increases, striving to uphold the constant product of $h$ and $R$, which is a finite value represented by $2 S \rho g$. This shape prevents the liquid from overflowing. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956051/preptracker/questions/q_38603_explanation_1764956050716.png In $h$ increases $R$ adjusts such that $(h R)=$ constant. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: A thin stainless steel needle can lay floating on a still water surface.

Reason: The needle floats when the buoyancy force balances the weight of the object.
explanation: "A thin stainless steel needle placed on calm water can remain afloat because the upward force arising from surface tension counters the needle's weight." So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: Tiny drops of liquid resist deforming forces better than bigger drops.

Reason: Excess pressure inside a drop is directly proportional to surface tension and inversely to radius.
explanation:
When a liquid drop is placed on a glass plate, its shape is influenced by two forces: gravity and surface tension. For very small drops, the potential energy resulting from gravity is negligible when compared to the potential energy from surface tension. Therefore, in such cases, the drop's shape is solely determined by surface tension, resulting in a spherical shape.
$(\Delta P) \propto\left[\dfrac{\text { surface tension }}{\text { Radius }}\right]$
So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: A large soap bubble expands while a small bubble shrinks when they are connected to each other by a capillary tube.

Reason: The excess pressure inside a bubble (or drop) is inversely proportional to the radius.
explanation:
When a large soap bubble and a small bubble are connected by a capillary tube, the larger bubble will expand while the smaller one will shrink. The reason is also true. The excess pressure inside a bubble is indeed inversely proportional to its radius, which explains why the larger bubble expands.
Excess Pressure $(\Delta P) \propto \dfrac{1}{R}$
So correct option is (1).

question:

Reason: At this temperature, intermolecular forces for liquids and gases become equal. Liquid can expand without any restriction.
explanation: At critical temperature $\left(t_{c}{ }^{\circ} C\right)$, the intermolecular forces for liquids and gases become equal, allowing the liquid to expand without any restriction. Surface tension $T_{s}$ varies as shown https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956050/preptracker/questions/q_38567_explanation_1764956049925.png So correct option is (1).

question:
Assertion: The concept of surface tension is held only for liquids.

Reason: Surface tension occurs for gases.
explanation: Liquids form definite surface. Gases do not have surface. The intermolecular distance among gas molecules is considerably greater than that among liquid molecules. Consequently, the cohesive forces among gas molecules are very weak, whereas they are much stronger in liquids. Hence, the concept of surface tension applies to liquids but not to gases. So correct option is (3).

Reason: The change in surface tension of the liquid depends upon the degree of contamination of the impurity.
explanation: Insoluble impurities like detergent generally decrease the surface tension of a liquid. The extent of this decrease depends on the type and degree of contamination by the impurity. So correct option is (1).

question:

Reason: There is surface tension in above scenario.
explanation: In this scenario, atmospheric pressure is not a relevant factor because it exerts forces in all directions. The significant force at play between two glass surfaces in contact with water is the adhesive force. Adhesive forces pertain to interactions between two dissimilar substances, Here polar nature of water molecule plays role. Due to surface tension of water, glass plates try to stick together. Due to the extensive surface area of the glass slide, the resultant force is substantial. Large force is needed separate two glass plates connected by a thin film of water.So correct option is (1).

question:
Assertion: It is better to wash the clothes in cold soap solution.

Reason: The surface tension of cold solution is more than the surface tension of hot solution.
explanation: As temperature increases surface tension decreases. The soap solution has lower surface tension compared to regular water, and its surface tension decreases further when heated. Consequently, hot soap solution can spread over a larger surface area and exhibits increased wetting power. This property allows hot soap solution to penetrate and clean clothes more effectively than ordinary water. So correct option is (4).
