explanation: Statement A is true as viscosity does arise due to the cohesive forces between the fluid molecules which resist their relative motion. Statement B is false because viscosity affects all layers of a fluid in relative motion, not just the surface layer. Correct option is (2)

Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question: A solid metal sphere released in a vertical liquid column has attained terminal velocity in the downward direction. The magnitudes of viscous force, buoyant force and gravitational force acting on it are $F_{v}, F_{B}$ and $F_{W}$ respectively. Then, the correct relation between them is
explanation:
Terminal velocity: It is a velocity (highest) that is obtained by an object falling through liquid. $F_{B}$ is buoyant force, $F_{V}$ is viscous force and $F_{W}$ is gravitational force.
Among these $F_{W}$ only acts in downward direction.
At terminal velocity,
$\Rightarrow \quad F_{W}=F_{B}+F_{V}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: What is the terminal velocity of a rain drop of radius $0.02\,mm$? [Note that the coefficient of viscosity of air is $1.8 \times 10^{-5} {~N} / {m}^{2}$, density of water is $1000 {~kg} / {m}^{3}$. Use, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ and density of air can be neglected in comparison with density of water]
explanation:
We know that,
$v_{\text {Terminal }}=\dfrac{2 g r^{2}}{9 \eta}\left(\rho_{b}-\rho_{l}\right)$
${v_{{\text{Temminal }}}} = \left( \begin{gathered} \frac{2}{9} \times \frac{{10}}{{1.8 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} \times \hfill \\ {\left( {\frac{{0.02}}{{1000}}} \right)^2}(1000 - 0)m/s \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right)$
$v_{\text {Terminal }}=4.9 {~cm} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A liquid flows steadily through a cylindrical pipe having a radius $2\,R$ at a point $A$ and radius $R$ at point $B$ farther along the flow direction. If the velocity at point $B$ is $4\,v$, what will be the velocity at point $A$ ?
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952644/preptracker/questions/q_109833_explanation_1764952643436.png According to principle of continuity,
$A_{1} v_{1}=A_{2} v_{2} \Rightarrow v_{1}=\dfrac{A_{2} v_{2}}{A_{1}}=\dfrac{\pi R_{2}^{2} \times 4 v}{\pi R_{1}^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{\pi R^{2} \times 4 v}{\pi(2 R)^{2}}=\dfrac{4 \pi R^{2} v}{4 \pi R^{2}}=v$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Poiseuille’s Law and Rate of Flow of Fluid through a Capillary Tube

question: A metal cube of side $10\,cm$ rests on a film of a liquid of thickness $0.2\,mm$. If upon applying a horizontal force of magnitude $0.1\,N$, the cube slides with a constant speed of $0.08\,m / s$, then the coefficient of viscosity is
explanation:
We know that, viscous force
$F=\eta \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta x} \cdot A \Rightarrow 0.1=\eta \times \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-4}} \times 10^{-2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 0.1=\eta \times 4 \Rightarrow \eta=\dfrac{0.1}{4}=0.025\,N-s / m^{2}$
$=2.5 \times 10^{-2} N-s / m^{2}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: What is the terminal velocity of an air bubble of $1.0\,mm$ in diameter rising in liquid of viscosity $0.85 {~N}-s / {m}^{2}$ and density $900\,kg{m^{ - 3}}$? (Take, air density $=1.293 {~kg} / {m}^{3}, g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
Let terminal velocity be $v_{T}$.
$\because \quad v_{T}=\dfrac{2}{9} r^{2} g\left(\dfrac{\rho-\sigma}{\eta}\right)$
$\therefore \quad v_{T}=\dfrac{2}{9} \times\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^{2} \times 10\left(\dfrac{900-1.293}{0.85}\right)$
$=\dfrac{2}{9} \times\left(\dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{2}\right)^{2} \times 10\left(\dfrac{900-1.293}{0.85}\right)$
$ = 587.4 \times {10^{ - 6}} = 0.587 \times {10^{ - 3}} = 0.587\,mm{s^{ - 1}}$
$ \simeq 0.5\,mm{s^{ - 1}}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: Match the columns I and columns II.
explanation:
A. Viscous force acts on any spherical body moving in viscous fluid.
According to Stoke's law,
Viscous force $(F) \propto$ Coefficient of viscosity ($\eta$)
$\propto$ Radius of body $(R)$
$\propto$ Velocity of body ($v$)
$F \propto \eta R v$
$F=6 \pi \eta R v$
$\therefore$ Stokes' law is related with viscous drag
B. Turbulence is related with Reynold's numberIf $R < 2000 \rightarrow$ laminar flow
$2000 < R < 3000 \rightarrow$ transition flow (both laminar and turbulent)
$R>3000 \rightarrow$ Turbulent flow
C. Bernoulli's principle
According to this principle,
$P+\rho g h+\dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{2}=$ constant
where, $P$ is pressure energy per unit volume,
$\rho g h$ is potential energy per unit volume and
$\dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{2}$ is kinetic energy per unit volume.
D. Pascal's law
In closed system, pressure applied to any part of the liquid is transmitted undiminished every where.
This principle is used in hydraulic lift.
Hence, ${\text{A}} \to {\text{R,B}} \to {\text{S,C}} \to {\text{P}}$, and ${\text{D}} \to {\text{Q}}$ are correct.
So, the correct option is (1).

options.text: A - R, B - S, C - P,  D - Q
A - P, B - Q, C - R, D - S
A - Q B - P, C - S,  D - R
A - R, B - S , C - Q  D - P

subject: Physics

question: The speed of the water in a river is $v$ near the surface. If the coefficient of viscosity of water is $\eta$ and the depth of the river is $H$, then the shearing stress between the horizontal layers of water is
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952641/preptracker/questions/q_109815_explanation_1764952641219.png Force of viscous drag is given by
$F=\eta A\left(\dfrac{d v}{d x}\right)$
In given case, $\dfrac{d v}{d x}=\dfrac{v}{H}$
So, shearing stress $=\dfrac{F}{A}=\eta \dfrac{d v}{d x}=\eta \cdot \dfrac{v}{H}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A copper ball of radius $3.0\,mm$ falls in an oil tank of viscosity $1 {~kg} / {ms}$. The terminal velocity of the copper ball will be (Density of oil $=1.5 \times 10^{3} {~kg} / {m}^{3}$, Density of copper $=9 \times 10^{3} {~kg} / {m}^{3}$ and $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$.)
explanation:
Terminal velocity, $v_{T}=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^{2}(\sigma-\rho) g}{\eta}$
Putting the given values, we get
$ = \frac{2}{9} \times \frac{{{{\left( {3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)}^2}\left( \begin{gathered} 9 \times {10^3} - \hfill \\ 1.5 \times {10^3} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right) \times 10}}{1}\,m/s$
$=\dfrac{2}{9} \times 9 \times 10^{-6} \times 7.5 \times 10^{3} \times 10 {~m} / {s}$
$\Rightarrow v_{T}=15 \times 10^{-2} {~m} / {s}$
Hence, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: What is the rate at which a trapped bubble of $2\,mm$ diameter rises through a solution of density $13.6 \times 10^{3} {~kg} / {m}^{3}$ and coefficient of viscosity 1.5 centipoise? Assume, the density of air is negligible and $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$.
explanation:
From stoke's law viscous drag acting on the bubble is given by $F_{D}=6 \pi \eta r v_{T}$
where, $\eta=$ Coefficient of viscosity
$=1.5 \times 10^{-2}$ poise
$=1.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} {~N}-{s} / {m}^{2}$
$=1.5 \times 10^{-3} {~N}-{s} / {m}^{2}$
$r=$ radius of bubble $=1 {~mm}=10^{-3} {~mm}$.
$v_{T}=$ Terminal velocity of bubble.
Buoyant force acting on the bubble is,
$F_{B}=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} p \cdot g$.
where $\rho=$ density of liquid
$=13.6 \times 10^{3} {~kg} / {m}^{3}$.
$\therefore \quad F_{D}=F_{B} \Rightarrow 6 \pi \eta r v_{T}=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \rho g$
$\Rightarrow v_{T}=\dfrac{2 \rho g r^{2}}{9 \eta}$
$\therefore v_{T}=\dfrac{2 \times 13.6 \times 10^{3} \times 10 \times\left(10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{9 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}} {~m} / {s}$
$=20 {~m} / {s}$.
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: While determining the coefficient of viscosity of a given liquid, a spherical steel ball sinks by a distance $h = 0.9\,m$. The radius of the ball is $r = \sqrt 3  \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m$. The time taken by the ball to sink in three trials are tabulated as follows. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952633/preptracker/questions/q_109791_question_1764952632695.png The difference between the densities of the steel  ball and the liquid is $7000\,kg\,{m^{ - 3}}.$ If $g = 10.0\,m{s^{ - 2}},$ then the coefficient of viscosity of  the given liquid at room temperature is
explanation:
Velocity, $v = \frac{{0.9}}{{2.7}} = \frac{1}{3}\,m/s$
Terminal velocity, of a sphere moving in a viscous fluid is,
$v_{T}=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta}$
Where $r=$ radius, $\rho=$ density of sphere,
$\sigma=$ density and $\eta=$ coefficient of viscosity of the fluid.
Now average time taken by the ball to fall by $0.9\,m$ is given by
$t=\dfrac{1}{3}(2.75+2.65+2.70) s=2.7 s$
$\therefore$ Average terminal velocity is
${v_T} = \frac{{0.9}}{{2.7}}\,m/s = \frac{1}{3}\,m/s$
$\therefore \eta=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{v_{T}}$
$ \Rightarrow \eta = \frac{2}{9} \times \frac{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}(7000) \times 10}}{{\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right)}} = 0.14\,Pa.s$
subject: Physics

question: A small spherical ball of radius ' $r$ ' falling through a viscous medium of negligible density has terminal velocity ' $v$ '. Another ball of the same mass but of radius $2 r$; falling through the same viscous medium will have terminal velocity.
explanation:
Terminal velocity of the ball through a viscous medium,
$v=\dfrac{2}{9} \times \dfrac{g}{\eta}(\rho-\sigma) r^{2}$
For given viscous medium, $\sigma=0$
$v=\dfrac{2}{9} \times \dfrac{g}{\eta} \rho r^{2} \Rightarrow v=\dfrac{2}{9} \times \dfrac{g}{\eta} \times \dfrac{m}{\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}} \times r^{2}$
i.e., $v \propto \dfrac{1}{r}$
For the $2^{\text {nd }}$ ball $r^{\prime} \rightarrow 2 r$
So, $\dfrac{v^{\prime}}{v}=\dfrac{r}{r^{\prime}} ; \dfrac{v^{\prime}}{v}=\dfrac{1}{2 r} \times \dfrac{r}{1}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow v^{\prime}=\dfrac{v}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A spherical ball of radius $1 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,m$ and density  ${10^5}\,kg/{m^3}$  falls freely under gravity through a distance $h$ before entering a tank of water. If after entering in water the velocity of the ball does not change, then the value of $h$ is approximately (The coefficient of viscosity of water is  $9.8 \times {10^{ - 6}}Ns/{m^2}{\mkern 1mu} )$
explanation:
The terminal velocity is given by:
$v=\dfrac{2}{9} r^{2} \dfrac{(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta}$
$v = \frac{2}{9} \times \frac{{{{\left( {{{10}^{ - 4}}} \right)}^2}\left( {{{10}^5} - {{10}^3}} \right) \times 10}}{{9.8 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 224.5\,m/s$
Velocity of ball just before entering the water tank is
$v = \sqrt {2gh} $
$ \Rightarrow h = \frac{{{v^2}}}{{2g}};h = \frac{{{{(224.5)}^2}}}{{2 \times 10}} = 2518\,m$
subject: Physics

question: A small ball of mass $m$ and density $\rho$ is dropped in a viscous liquid of density $\rho_{0}$. After sometime, the ball falls with constant velocity. The viscous force on the ball is
explanation:
The small ball is having mass $m$ and density $\rho$. Let the mass of liquid displaced be $m^{\prime}$.
Volume of ball is $V=\dfrac{m}{\rho}$ $\quad \quad (1)$
Volume of liquid displaced by ball,
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957404/preptracker/questions/q_39312_explanation_1764957403881.png
$V^{\prime}=\dfrac{m^{\prime}}{\rho_{0}}$ $\quad \quad (2)$
Volume of ball = Volume of liquid displaced
$\dfrac{m}{\rho}=\dfrac{m^{\prime}}{\rho_{0}}$
$m^{\prime}=\dfrac{m}{\rho} \rho_{0}$ $\quad \quad (3)$
Buoyant force, $F_{B}=\rho_{0} V^{\prime} g=m^{\prime} g$
Viscous force on the ball is, $F_{V}=m g-m^{\prime} g$.
$F_{V}=m g-\left(\dfrac{m}{\rho} \rho_{0}\right) g=m g\left(1-\dfrac{\rho_{0}}{\rho}\right)$
subject: Physics

question: A thin plate ${A B}$ of large area ${A}$ is placed symmetrically in a small gap of height ' ${h}$ ' filled with water of viscosity ${\eta_{0}}$ and the plate has a constant velocity ${v}$ by applying a force ${F}$, as shown in the figure. If the gap is filled with some other liquid of viscosity ${0.75 \eta_{0}}$ where should the plate be placed in the gap, so that the plate can again be pulled at the same constant velocity ${V}$, by applying the same force ${F}$. (Take ${h=20 {~cm}}$)
explanation:
Case I: ${y_{1}=y_{2}=h / 2}$
$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}=\dfrac{v}{h / 2}=\dfrac{2 v}{h}$
Viscous force ${=\dfrac{\eta_{0} 2 {~V}}{h} {~A}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957034/preptracker/questions/q_39114_explanation_1764957033962.png The plate has got two sides.
Total force ${F=\dfrac{4 \eta_{c} v a}{h}}$
Case II: ${F=\dfrac{\eta v A}{y}+\dfrac{\eta v A}{y-h}=\dfrac{\eta v A h}{y(h-y)}}$
$\dfrac{\eta v A h}{y(h-y)}=\dfrac{4 n^{2} A v}{h} ; \dfrac{3}{16} h^{2}=y(h-y)$
Solving we get ${y=\dfrac{h}{4}, \dfrac{3 h}{4}}$
Hence, for minimum distance from top ${y=20 / 4=5 {~cm}}$
subject: Physics

question:
A metal plate of area $0.2\,{m^2}$ is connected to a $0.01\,kg$ of mass via a string that passes over an ideal pulley. A liquid with a film thickness of $0.4\,\,mm$ is placed between the plate and the table as shown in figure above. When released, the plate moves to the right with a constant speed of $0.08\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ The coefficient of viscosity of the liquid is $n \times {10^{ - 3}}Pa - s.$ Find $n$.
(Take $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$)
explanation:
The metal plate will move under the action of force,
$F = mg = 0.01 \times 10\,N\,$
As, $F = \eta \,\,A\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}$
$\therefore \quad \eta = \frac{{Fdx}}{{A\,\,dv}} = \frac{{0.01 \times 10 \times 4 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{0.2 \times 0.08}}$
$\therefore \quad \eta = \frac{1}{{400}} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,Pa - s$
subject: Physics

question: The radius of a pipe carrying a liquid decreases by $20 \%$ because of deposits on the inner surface. By how many times would the pressure difference between the ends of the constricted pipe should be increased to original pressure difference to maintain a constant flow rate?
explanation:
Let $l$ and $r$ be the length and radius of the pipe. If $P$ is the pressure difference between the two ends of the pipe, then the rate of flow of the liquid through the pipe,
$V = \frac{{\pi {{\Pr }^4}}}{{8\eta l}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Let the pressure difference be changed to $P',$ when the radius of the pipe decreases by $20 \%$
i.e., becomes $r' = 0.8r$
$\therefore \quad V = \frac{{\pi P'{r^4}}}{{8\eta l}} = \frac{{\pi P' \times {{(0.8r)}^4}}}{{8\eta l}}$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From the equations (1) and (2)
$\frac{{\pi P' \times {{(0.8r)}^4}}}{{8\eta l}} = \frac{{\pi {{\Pr }^4}}}{{8\eta l}}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{{P'}}{P} = \frac{{{r^4}}}{{{{(0.8r)}^4}}} = \frac{1}{{{{(0.8)}^4}}}$
$ = \frac{1}{{0.64}} \times \frac{1}{{0.64}}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{{P'}}{P} = \frac{{25}}{{16}} \times \frac{{25}}{{16}} = \frac{{625}}{{256}} = 2.44$
$\therefore \quad P' = 2.44$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Poiseuille’s Law and Rate of Flow of Fluid through a Capillary Tube

question: The ratio of radii of two spheres are ${1: 2}$ when they are dropped in a viscous liquid. The ratio of their terminal speed is
explanation:
Terminal velocity is directly proportional to the square of radius of the body.
${\therefore v \propto r^{2}}$
$\frac{{{v_1}}}{{{v_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{r_1}}}{{{r_2}}}} \right)^2} = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{1}{4}\,\,\,\left[ {\because \frac{{{r_2}}}{{{r_1}}} = \frac{1}{2}{\text{ Given}}} \right]$.
So correct option is (4)
subject: Physics

question: The velocity of rain drop having radius 1 $mm$ is ${20 {~cm} {~s}^{-1}}$. The velocity of raindrops of size 3 $mm$ is
explanation:
Terminal velocity, ${v_{t}=\dfrac{2 r^{2}(\rho-\sigma)}{9 \eta} g}$,
where, ${\rho=}$ density of liquid, ${\sigma=}$ density of air/medium and ${\eta=}$ coefficient of viscosity of the liquid.
${\Rightarrow v_{t} \propto r^{2}}$
${r_{1}=1 {~mm}=10^{-3} {~m}, v_{1}=20 \times 10^{-2} {~m} / {s}}$
${r_2} = 3\,mm = 3 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m,{v_2} = ?$
${\dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{3}{1}\right)^{2}}$
${\Rightarrow v_{2}=9 v_{1}=9 \times 20 \times 10^{-2} {~m} / {s}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 180\,cm/s$.
So correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: A cylindrical vessel filled with water is released on an inclined surface of angle $\theta$ as shown in figure.The friction coefficient of surface with the vessel is $\mu \left( { < \tan \,\theta } \right).$ Then the contact angle made by the surface of water with the incline will be
explanation: Figure shows forces acting on a particle on the surface, with respect to vessel. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956954/preptracker/questions/q_38890_explanation_1764956953665.png $\tan \,\theta  = \mu \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\theta  = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\mu $
subject: Physics

question: The terminal velocity of small sized spherical body of radius $r$ falling vertically in a viscous liquid is given by the following proportionality
explanation:
According to formula,
$v=\dfrac{2 r^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{9 \eta}$
Hence, $v \propto r^{2}$
subject: Physics

question: Two equal drops of water are falling through air with a steady velocity $v$. If the drops coalesced what will be the new velocity?
explanation:
Let $r$ be the radius of the each drop. The terminal velocity of drop will be given by
$v = \frac{2}{9}\frac{{{r^2}(\rho - \sigma )g}}{\eta }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
where, $\rho$ is density of drop and $\sigma$ is density of viscous medium of coefficient of viscosity $\eta$. When two drops each of radius $r$ coalesce to form a new drop, then the radius of coalesced drop will be
$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \times 2 & =\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} \\\Rightarrow \quad R & =(2)^{1 / 3} r\end{aligned}$
Hence, new terminal velocity of coalesced drop will be
$\begin{equation*}v^{\prime}=\dfrac{2}{9}\left[\dfrac{\left(2^{1 / 3} r\right)^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta}\right] \tag{2}\end{equation*}$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{v^{\prime}}{v}=(2)^{2 / 3} \\& v^{\prime}=(2)^{2 / 3} v\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: An air bubble of radius $1\,cm$ rises from the bottom portion through a liquid of density $1.5\,g\,c{c^{ - 1}}$ at a constant speed of  $0.25{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} cm\,{s^{ - 1}}.$ If the density of air is neglected, the coefficient of viscosity of the liquid is approximately (in $P a$ ).
explanation:
Terminal velocity,
$v=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^{2} \rho g}{\eta} \Rightarrow \eta=\dfrac{2}{9} \cdot \dfrac{r^{2} \rho g}{v}$
$=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2} \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{3}\right) \times 9.8}{0.25 \times 10^{-2}}$
$=130 \mathrm{~Pa}$
subject: Physics

question: Two raindrops of same radius $r$ falling with terminal velocity $v$ merge and from a bigger drop of radius $R$. The terminal velocity of the bigger drop is
explanation:
Terminal velocity, $v=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta}$
$\therefore \quad v \propto r^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{v}{V}=\dfrac{r^{2}}{R^{2}} \Rightarrow V=\dfrac{v R^{2}}{r^{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: What is the velocity $v$ of a metallic ball of radius $r$ falling in a tank of liquid at the instant when its acceleration is one-half that of a freely falling body?(The densities of metal and of liquid are $\rho$ and $\sigma$, respectively and the viscosity of liquid is $\eta$ )
explanation:
Net force on the ball = downward
force - upward force $=\dfrac{m g}{2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956686/preptracker/questions/q_38822_explanation_1764956685943.png ${\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}(\rho - \sigma )g - 6\pi \eta rv = \frac{{mg}}{2}}$
${\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}(\rho - \sigma )g - 6\pi \eta rv = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}\rho } \right)g}$
${r^3}(\rho - \sigma )g - \frac{9}{2}\eta v = \frac{1}{2}{r^2}\rho g$
$\frac{9}{2}v\eta = {r^2}(\rho - \sigma )g - \frac{1}{2}{r^2}\rho g$
$ = \frac{1}{2}{r^2}\rho g - {r^2}\sigma g$
$\frac{9}{2}v\eta = {r^2}g(\rho - 2\,\sigma )$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,v = \frac{{{r^2}g}}{{9\eta }}(\rho - 2\,\sigma )$
subject: Physics

question: A rain drop of radius $0.3\,mm$ has a terminal velocity in air $1\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ The viscosity of air is $18 \times 10^{-5}$ poise. Find the viscous force on the rain drops.
explanation:
Here,
$r = 0.3\,mm = 0.3 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m,v = 1\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
$\eta=18 \times 10^{-5}$ poise
$=18 \times 10^{-6}$ deca poise
Viscous force, $F=6 \pi \eta r v$
$=6 \times \dfrac{22}{7} \times\left(18 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(0.3 \times 10^{-3}\right) \times 1$
$ = 1.018 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,N$
subject: Physics

question: A space $2.5\,cm$ wide between two large plane surfaces is filled with oil. Force required to drag a very thin plate of area ${0.5 {~m}^{2}}$ just midway the surfaces at a speed of ${0.5 {~m} / {s}}$ is $1\,N.$   The coefficient of viscosity in ${{kg}-s / {m}^{2}}$ is
explanation:
Velocity gradient ${=\dfrac{0.5 \times 2}{2.5 \times 10^{-2}}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \text { Also, } F=2 \eta A \dfrac{d v}{d z}=2 \times \eta \times(0.5) \times \dfrac{0.5}{1.25 \times 10^{-2}} \\& \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta=2.5 \times 10^{-2} {~kg}-{sec} / {m}^{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A rain drop of radius $1,5\,mm$ experiences a drag force ${F=\left(2 \times 10^{-5} {v}\right) {N}}$, while falling through air from a height 2 km with a velocity ${v}$. The terminal velocity of the rain drop will be nearly (use ${g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$ )
explanation:
At equilibrium,
weight of raindrop ${=}$ Drag force on the spherical raindrop
$\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \rho g=2 \times 10^{-5} v$
${v_T} = \frac{{\left( \begin{array}{l} 4 \times (3.14) \times {(1.5)^3} \times \\ {\left( {{{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)^3} \times {10^3} \times 10 \end{array} \right)}}{{3 \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = 7\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark the correct options given below:

Assertion: A spherical body of radius $(5 \pm 0.1)mm$ having a particular density is falling through a liquid of constant density. The percentage error in the calculation of its terminal velocity is $4 \%$.

Reason: The terminal velocity of the spherical body falling through the liquid is inversely proportional to its radius.
explanation:
The expression for terminal velocity is given by,
${v_T} = \frac{2}{9}\frac{{{r^2}}}{\eta }\left( {\rho - {\rho _0}} \right)g\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Where, $r \rightarrow$ radius of particle.
$\rho \rightarrow$ density of particle
$\rho_{0} \rightarrow$ density of fluid
$\eta \rightarrow$ coefficient of viscosity
Percentage error in terminal velocity is,
$\dfrac{\Delta v}{v} \times 100=\dfrac{2 \Delta r}{r} \times 100=\dfrac{2(0.1)}{5} \times 100=4 \%$
From equation (1) $v_{T} \propto r^{2}$
So, reason statement is incorrect.
Hence, Assertion is true but Reason is false.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: Eight equal drops of water are falling through air with a steady speed of $10\,cm/s$. If the drops coalesce, the new velocity is
explanation:
Terminal velocity, $v_{T}=\dfrac{2 r^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{9 \eta}$
i.e., $v_{T} \propto r^{2} (\because$ All other quantities are constant $)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{T}}{v_{T}^{\prime}}=\dfrac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}$
As volume remains constant
$\therefore$ Volume of the big drop $=8 \times$ volume of each small drop
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=8 \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}$
$\Rightarrow R=2 r$
$\therefore \dfrac{10}{v_{T}^{\prime}}=\dfrac{r^{2}}{(2 r)^{2}}=\dfrac{r^{2}}{4 r^{2}}$
$v_T^\prime = 40\,cm/s$
So, correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Water flows faster than honey.

Reason: The coefficient of viscosity of water is less than honey.
explanation: Water has a lower coefficient of viscosity compared to honey. It means it offers less resistance to flow. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: All the raindrops hit the surface of the earth with a different constant velocity.

Reason: An object falling through a viscous medium eventually attains a terminal velocity.
explanation: When rain drops of varying sizes fall through gravity, they eventually reach their terminal velocities $v_{t}=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{g r^{2}\left(\rho-\rho_{\text {air }}\right)}{\eta}$. The viscous drag of air is playing role. The terminal velocity of a drop is proportional to the square of its radius. So, a larger raindrop will have a greater terminal velocity and therefore fall faster than a smaller raindrop. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The velocity of fall of a man jumping with a parachute first increases and then becomes constant.

Reason: The constant velocity of fall of man is called terminal velocity.
explanation: As a person jumps from a height into the air while using a parachute, inertia of motion plays role. The velocity initially increases due to the dominant gravitational force, which overcomes the opposing forces of air resistance (viscous drag) and buoyancy. As the person's velocity continues to rise, the air resistance (viscous drag) also increases as $F_{\text {viscous }} \propto v$. Finally, a point is reached where the air resistance and buoyancy forces counteract the gravitational force. At this stage, the person descends with a constant velocity, which is called to as the terminal velocity. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: Two small spherical drops having radii in the ratio $4: 3$ fall from a height through the atmosphere. Their momenta ratio on reaching the earth has the value
explanation:
$P=m v$
Terminal Velocity $v \propto r^{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{16}{9}$
mass $=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \rho \Rightarrow m \propto r^{3}$
$\therefore$ Momentums are in ratio
$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{p_{1}}{p_{2}} & =\dfrac{r_{1}^{3}}{r_{2}^{3}} \times \dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right)^{3} \times \dfrac{16}{9} \\& =\dfrac{64}{27}=\dfrac{16}{9}=\dfrac{1024}{243}=4: 21\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: If the terminal speed of a sphere of gold (density $\left. { = 19.5 \times {{10}^3}\,kg{\rm{/}}{m^3}} \right)$ is $0.5\,m{\rm{/}}s$ in a viscous liquid $\left(\right.$ density$\left. { = 3\,kg{\rm{/}}{m^3}} \right)$. Find the terminal velocity of a sphere of silver (density $=10.5 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ ) of the same size in the same liquid.
explanation:
$V_{T}=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^{3}(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta} \rho=$ density of sphere
$\sigma=$ density of liquid
$\therefore V \propto(\rho-\sigma) \Rightarrow \dfrac{V_{g}}{V_{s}}=\dfrac{\rho_{g}-\sigma}{\rho_{s}-\sigma}$
$\dfrac{0.5}{V_{s}}=\dfrac{19.5-3}{10.5-3}=\dfrac{16.5}{7.5} \Rightarrow V_{s}=\dfrac{0.5 \times 7.5}{16.5}$
${V_s} = 0.22\,m{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics

question: Ten drops of a liquid of density $\rho$ and each of radius $r$ are falling through air with a terminal velocity $12\,cm{\rm{/}}s$. When the 8 drops out of 10 drops coalesce to from a single drop, the terminal velocity of the new drop will be
explanation:
Eight drops coalesce to form one drop of radius $\mathrm{R}$.
Volume of Big drop$ = \frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Volume of each smaller drop $ = \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From equations (1) & (2) we get
$\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=8 \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \Rightarrow R=2 r$
For a liquid drop
${v_r} \propto {r^2} \Rightarrow \frac{{{v_r}}}{{{v_R}}} = {\left( {\frac{r}{{2r}}} \right)^2} = \frac{1}{4}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_R} = 4 \times 12 = 48\,cm\,{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: A square plate of $0.5\,m$ side length parallel to a second plate with a velocity of $0.5\,m{\rm{/}}s$, both plates being immersed in water. If the viscous force is $0.081\,N$ and the coefficient of viscosity is 0.02 poise, distance between the plates in $cm$ is
explanation:
$F=\eta \dfrac{A d v}{d x} \Rightarrow d x=\eta \dfrac{A d v}{F}$
$=\dfrac{0.002 \times 0.25 \times 0.5}{0.081}$
$\therefore dx = 0.00308\,m = 0.308\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A layer of glycerin of thinkness $3\,mm$ is present between a large surface and a surface of area of $0.2\,{m^2}$, with what force the small surface is to be pulled, so that it can move with a velocity of $5\,m{\rm{/}}s$? $\left( {\eta  = 0.09\,kg{m^{ - 1}}\,{s^{ - 1}}} \right)$.
subject: Physics

question: A spherical body falling through a viscous liquid of infinite extent ultimately attains a constant value of velocity then
explanation: Viscous drag and upthrust acts upward and weight acts downward for constant velocity (terminal velocity). Viscous drag to liquid and upthrust should balance weight of the ball.
subject: Physics

question:
The velocity of the surface layer of water in a river of depth $20\,m$ is $5\,m{\rm{/}}s$. The shearing stress between the surface layer and the bottom layer
is $\left( {\eta = {{10}^{ - 3}}poise} \right)$
explanation:
Velocity gradient or strain rate in liquid
$\eta=\dfrac{\text { shearing stress }}{\text { strain rate }}$
$\Rightarrow$ shearing stress $=\eta \times$ strain rate
Shearing stress $=10^{-4} \times \dfrac{5}{20}=0.25 \times 10^{-4}$
$ = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,N{\rm{/}}{m^2}$
subject: Physics

question: Eight identical small drops of water are falling down vertically through a medium, each with terminal velocity ' $V$ '. If they combine to form a single drop, then its terminal velocity will be
explanation:
Let radius of each drop $=r$
Terminal velocity, $V=\dfrac{2}{g} \dfrac{r^{2}}{\eta}(\rho-\sigma) g$
Let ' $R$ ' be the radius of big drop.
Volume of big drop $=$ volume of 8 small drops
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=8 \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \Rightarrow R=2 r$
Let $V^{\prime}$ be the terminal velocity of bigger drop,
$V^{\prime}=\dfrac{2 R^{2}(\rho-\sigma)}{9 \eta}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{V^{\prime}}{V}=\dfrac{R^{2}}{r^{2}}=\dfrac{(2 r)^{2}}{r^{2}}=4 \Rightarrow V^{\prime}=4 V$
subject: Physics

question: If the terminal speed of a sphere of gold (density $\left. { = 19.5kg{m^{ - 3}}} \right)$ is $0.2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ in viscous liquid $\left( {density = 1.5{\mkern 1mu} kg{m^{ - 3}}} \right)$, find the terminal speed of a sphere of silver (density $=10.5 \mathrm{kgm}^{-3}$) of the same size in the same liquid.
explanation:
Terminal speed of spherical body in a viscous liquid is given by
$v_{T}=\dfrac{2 r^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{9 \eta}$
where, $\rho=$ density of material of solid body and $\sigma=$ density of liquid.
From given data
$\dfrac{v_{T}(\text { Ag })}{v_{T}(\text { Gold })}=\dfrac{\rho_{\text {Ag }}-\sigma_{1}}{\rho_{\text {Gold }}-\sigma_{1}}$
(Since size $r$ and $\eta$ of liquid are same for both spheres)
$ \Rightarrow {v_T}(Ag) = \left( {\frac{{10.5 - 1.5}}{{19.5 - 1.5}}} \right) \times 0.2$
$ = \frac{9}{{18}} \times 0.2 = 0.1\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
So, correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: Two tubes of radii $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ and lengths $l_{1}$ and $l_{2}$, respectively, are connected in series and a liquid flows through each of them in stream line conditions. $P_{1}$ and $P_{2}$ are pressure differences across the two tubes. If $P_{2}$ is $4 P_{1}$ and $l_{2}$ is $\dfrac{l_{1}}{4}$, then the radius $r_{2}$ will be equal to -
explanation:
$Q=\dfrac{\pi}{8} \dfrac{p r^{4}}{\eta L}$
The rate of flow is same in both tubes
$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{p_{1} r_{1}^{4}}{L_{1}} & =\dfrac{p_{2} r_{2}^{4}}{L_{2}} \\\dfrac{p_{1} r_{1}^{4}}{L_{1}} & =\dfrac{4 p_{1} r_{2}^{4}}{\dfrac{L_{1}}{4}} \Rightarrow r_{2}^{4}=\dfrac{r_{1}^{4}}{16} \\r_{2} & =\dfrac{r_{1}}{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Rate of Flow of Fluid through a Combination of Capillary Tubes

question: Which of the following option correctly describes the variation of the speed \[v\] and acceleration ' \(a\) ' of a point mass falling vertically in a viscous medium that applies a force \[F = - kv\], where '\[k\] ' is constant, on the body ? (Graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
explanation:
The acceleration of the body is
$a = g - \alpha v\left( {\alpha = \frac{K}{m}} \right)$
$\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = g - \alpha v$
$\int_0^v {\frac{{dv}}{{g - \alpha v}}} $
$ = \int_0^t {dt} \Rightarrow \ln \left( {\frac{{g - \alpha v}}{v}} \right) = - \alpha t$
$v = {v_0}\left( {1 - {e^{ - \alpha t}}} \right),\quad a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = {v_0}\alpha {e^{ - \alpha t}}$
where $v_{0}=\dfrac{g}{\alpha}$
subject: Physics

question: The velocity of water in a river is $18\,km/hr$ near the surface. If the river is $5\,m$ deep, find the shearing stress between the horizontal layers of water. The co-efficient of viscosity of water $=10^{-2}$ poise.
explanation:
$\eta = {10^{ - 2}}{\rm{ }}poise{\rm{ }} = {10^{ - 3}}\frac{N}{{{m^2}}}\,s$
$v = 18\,km/h = \frac{{18000}}{{3600}} = 5\,m/s$
$l = 5\,m$
Strain rate $=\dfrac{v}{l}$
Coefficient of viscosity, $\eta=\dfrac{\text { shearing stress }}{\text { strain rate }}$
$\therefore$ Shearing strees $=\eta \times$ strain rate
$ = {10^{ - 3}} \times \frac{5}{5} = {10^{ - 3}}N{m^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics

question: In an experiment, a small steel ball falls through a liquid at a constant speed of  $10\,cm/s$. If the steel ball is pulled upward with a force equal to twice its effective weight, how fast will it move upward?
explanation:
The forces exerted on the steel ball are shown in the figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956363/preptracker/questions/q_38155_explanation_1764956363176.png
where $F_{g}-F_{b}$ is the effective force on the sphere.
$6\pi \eta rv = {F_g} - {F_b}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$6\pi \eta r{v^\prime } + {F_g} - {F_b} = 2\left( {{F_g} - {F_b}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From the above two equations
(put (1) in (2))
$ \Rightarrow {v^\prime } = v = 10\,cm/s$
subject: Physics

question: A small iron sphere is dropped from a great height. It attains its terminal velocity after having fallen $32\,m$. Then, it covers the rest of the path with terminal velocity only. The work done by air friction during the first $32\,m$ of fall is $W_{1}$. The work done by air friction during the subsequent $32\,m$ fall is $W_{2}$. Then
explanation:
In both cases the sphere travels same distance. The viscous force in the first part gradually increases from zero and reaches maximum. In the second part the the maximum viscous force acts throughout the motion. So work done in second part is more than the work done in the first part.
$W_{2}>W_{1}$
subject: Physics

question: In the figure shown, a thin disc of radius $R$ is is located on the surface of an oil filled container. The distance between the disc and the lower base of the container is $D$. The disc is driven at constant angular speed $\omega$ about the axis passing through the centre of the disc. The viscosity of the oil is $\eta$. Find the torque required to drive the disc.
explanation:
The top view of the disc is shown in the figure. Consider a circular slice having radius $r$ and with $d r$. The linear velocity of any point on the slice is $r \omega$.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956027/preptracker/questions/q_38130_explanation_1764956027318.png
The small viscous force on the circular slice is
$d f=\eta d A \dfrac{v}{D}=\eta(2 \pi r d r) \dfrac{r \omega}{D}$
The torque
$\begin{aligned}d \tau=d f r=\dfrac{2 \pi \eta \omega r^{3} d r}{D} \Rightarrow \tau & =\dfrac{2 \pi \eta \omega}{D} \int_{0}^{R} r^{3} d r \\& =\dfrac{\pi \eta \omega R^{4}}{2 D}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Figure shows a long solid cylinder of radius $R$ inside a long hollow tube of inner radius $R^{\prime}$. A viscous liquid of coefficient of viscosity $\eta$ is filled in the gap between the cylinder and the hollow tube. If the solid cylinder is moved with velocity $v$ parallel to its length inside the fixed hollow tube, find the force required per unit length to be applied on the solid cylinder, assuming uniform velocity gradient in the liquid.
explanation:
The viscous force on the cylinder is
$\begin{aligned}& F=-\eta A \dfrac{d v}{d x}=-(2 \pi R h \eta) \dfrac{v-0}{R-R^{\prime}} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{F}{h}=\eta(2 \pi R) \dfrac{v}{R^{\prime}-R}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A tube of length $l$ and radius $R$ carries a steady flow of fluid whose density is $\rho$ and viscosity $\eta$. The fluid flow velocity depends on the distance $r$ from the axis of the tube as $v=v_{0}\left(1-r^{2} / R^{2}\right)$. Find the mass of the fluid flowing across the section of the tube per unit time.
explanation:
Consider the cross-section view of the tube.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956345/preptracker/questions/q_38121_explanation_1764956344112.png
The small rate of flow of liquid across the circular of radius $r$ and width $d r$ is
$d Q=\rho(d A) v$
$\begin{aligned}& d Q=\rho(2 \pi r) d r v=2 \pi \rho v_{0}\left(r-\dfrac{r^{3}}{R^{2}}\right) d r \\& Q=2 \pi \rho v_{0}\left[\int_{0}^{R} r d r-\dfrac{1}{R^{2}} \int_{0}^{R} r^{3} d r\right] \\& =2 \pi \rho v_{0}\left[\dfrac{R^{2}}{2}-\dfrac{1}{R^{2}} \dfrac{R^{4}}{4}\right]=\dfrac{\pi \rho v_{0} R^{2}}{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Poiseuille’s Law and Rate of Flow of Fluid through a Capillary Tube

question: A spherical solid ball of volume $V$ is made of a material of density $\rho_{1}$. It is falling through a liquid of density $\rho_{2}\left(\rho_{2} < \rho_{1}\right)$. Assume that the liquid applies a viscous force on the ball that is proportional to the square of its speed $v$, i.e., $F_{\text {viscous }}=-k v^{2}(k>0)$. The terminal speed of the ball is
explanation:
The condition for terminal speed $\left(v_{t}\right)$ is
Weight $=$ Buoyant force + Viscous force
$\therefore \quad v \rho_{1} g=v \rho_{2} g+k v_{t}^{2}$
$\therefore v_{t}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\operatorname{Vg}\left(\rho_{1}-\rho_{2}\right)}{k}}$
subject: Physics

question: Spherical particles of pollen are shaken up in water and allowed to settle. The depth of water is $2 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$. What is the diameter of the largest particles remaining in suspension one hour later? Density of pollen $ = 1.8 \times {10^3}kg{m^{ - 3}}$, viscosity of water $=1 \times 10^{-2}$ poise and density of water $ = 1 \times {10^3}\,kg\,{m^3}$.
explanation:
After shaking different particles take different times to settle down. Here all the particles have to travel same distance. We assume that the particles reach terminal velocity immediately but they have different values because of different radii.
We know the terminal velocity $v=\dfrac{s}{t}$
$\therefore \dfrac{s}{t}=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta} \Rightarrow r^{2}=\dfrac{9 s}{2 t} \dfrac{\eta}{(\rho-\sigma) g}$
Given $s = 2 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m,t = 3600\,s$
$\therefore \quad \eta = 1 \times {10^{ - 2}}{\rm{poise }} = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,kg\,{m^{ - 1}}\,{s^{ - 1}}$
Substituting given values, we get
${r^2} = \frac{9}{2} \times \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{3600}} \times \frac{{1 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{\left( {1.8 \times {{10}^3} - 1 \times {{10}^3}} \right) \times 10}}$
$\therefore \quad r = \sqrt {\frac{{100}}{{32}}} \times {10^{ - 6}}\,m = 1.77 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,m$
Diameter $D=2 r=2 \times 1.77 \mu m=3.54 \mu m$
subject: Physics

explanation:
$\Delta KE = 0,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{since}}\,v = $ constant
$KE = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
Mass is falling down so potential energy decrease at rate
$=m g\left(\dfrac{d h}{d t}\right)=m g v \quad[\text { decreases }]$
subject: Physics

question: A fluid with viscosity $\eta$ fills the space between two along co-axial cylinders of radii $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$. The inner cylinder is stationary while the outer one is rotated with a constant angular velocity $\omega_{2}$. The fluid flow is laminar. Taking into account that the friction force acting on a unit area of cylindrical surface radius $r$ is defined by the formula $\sigma=\eta r\left(\dfrac{d \omega}{d r}\right)$, find the angular velocity of rotating fluid as a function of radius $r$.
explanation:
Consider a thin cylindrical shell of radius $r$. Let $L$ is the length of the cylinder. The viscous force present between two adjacent cylinders separated by $d r$ and having radius $r$ is
$f=\eta 2 \pi r L r \dfrac{d \omega}{d r}$
The torque $\tau=\eta 2 \pi L r^{3} \dfrac{d \omega}{d r}$
$\int_{{R_2}}^r {\frac{{dr}}{{{r^3}}}} = \frac{{2\pi \eta L}}{\tau }\int_{{\omega _2}}^\omega d \omega \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\int_{{R_2}}^{{R_1}} {\frac{1}{{{r^3}}}} dr = \frac{{2\pi \eta L}}{\tau }\int_{{\omega _2}}^0 d \omega \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Dividing the two equations we get
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}-\dfrac{1}{R_{2}^{2}}}{\dfrac{1}{R_{1}^{2}}-\dfrac{1}{R_{2}^{2}}}=\dfrac{\omega-\omega_{2}}{-\omega_{2}} \\& \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_{2}}=1-\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}-\dfrac{1}{R_{2}^{2}}}{\dfrac{1}{R_{1}^{2}}-\dfrac{1}{R_{2}^{2}}} \\& \omega=\omega_{2}\left[\dfrac{1}{R_{1}^{2}}-\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}\right] \dfrac{R_{1}^{2} R_{2}^{2}}{R_{2}^{2}-R_{1}^{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A long cylinder of radius $R_{1}$ is displaced along its axis with a constant velocity $v_{0}$ inside a stationary co-axial cylinder of radius $R_{2}$ as shown in the figure. The space between the cylinders is filled with viscous liquid. Find the velocity of the liquid as a function of the distance $r$ from the axis of the cylinders.
explanation:
Let $v$ is the velocity of a spherical shell or radius $r$
The viscous force between two adjacent layers having radius $r$ is
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956306/preptracker/questions/q_38100_explanation_1764956306206.png
where $L$ is the length of the cylinder. $d v$ is the relative velocity between two layers which are separated by $d r$.
As we move towards right $v$ decreases and $r$ increases.
$dv = ( - )ve\,\& \,dr = ( + )ve$
$f = - 2\pi rL\eta \frac{{dv}}{{dr}}$
$\int_{{R_1}}^r {\frac{{dr}}{r}} = \frac{{ - 2\pi L\eta }}{f}\int_{{v_0}}^v d v$
$\ln \frac{r}{{{R_1}}} = \frac{{2\pi L\eta \left( {{v_0} - v} \right)}}{f}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\int_{{R_1}}^{{R_2}} {\frac{{dr}}{r}} = \frac{{ - 2\pi L\eta }}{f}\int_{{v_0}}^0 d v$
$\ln \frac{{{R_2}}}{{{R_1}}} = \frac{{2\pi L\eta {v_0}}}{f}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From the above two equations we get
$\dfrac{\ln \left(r / R_{1}\right)}{\ln \left(R_{2} / R_{1}\right)}=\left(\dfrac{v_{0}-v}{v_{0}}\right)$
$v=v_{0}\left[1-\dfrac{\ln \left(r / R_{1}\right)}{\ln \left(R_{2} / R_{1}\right)}\right]=\dfrac{v_{0} \ln \left(R_{2} / r\right)}{\ln \left(R_{2} / R_{1}\right)}$
subject: Physics

question: An air bubbles rises from te bottom of a lake of large depth. The rising speed of air bubble will
explanation: Velocity becomes constant when terminal velocity is attained.

options.text: go on increasing till it reaches surface
go on decreasing till it reaches surface
increase in the beginning then will become constant

subject: Physics

question: The ratio of the terminal velocities of two drops of radii $R$ and $R/2$ is
explanation:
${v_T} \propto {r^2}$
$\therefore \frac{{{{\left( {{v_T}} \right)}_1}}}{{{{\left( {{v_T}} \right)}_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{R}{{R/2}}} \right)^2} = 4$
subject: Physics

question: Eight equal drops of water are falling through air with a steady velocity of $10\,cm\,{s^{ - 1}}$. If the drops combine to form a single drop big in size, then the terminal velocity of this big drop is
explanation:
Let $$r$$ be radius of each small water droplet and $R$ be radius of a big drop.
Volume of big drop $$=8 \times$$ Volume of each
${\text{ small}}\,{\text{droplets}}\frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3}{\text{ }} = 8 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}R{\text{ }} = 2r$
Let terminal velocity of small droplet be $$v_{1}$$ and of big drop be $$v_{2}$$.
Terminal velocity, $$v$$ is
where the symbols have their usual meaning
$v\mu {r^2}$
$\frac{{{v_2}}}{{{v_1}}} = \frac{{r_2^2}}{{r_1^2}} = {\left( {\frac{{2r}}{r}} \right)^2} = 4$
$$\Rightarrow \quad v_{2}=4 v_{1}$$
or $ = 4 \times 10\,cm\,{s^{ - 1}} = 40\,cm\,{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: Two capillary tubes of the same length but different radii $$r_{1}$$ and $$r_{2}$$ are fitted in parallel to the bottom of a vessel. The pressure head is $$p$$. What should be the radius of a single tube that can replace the two tubes so that the rate of flow is same as before
explanation:
$$V=V_{1}+V_{2}$$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\pi p{r^4}}}{{8\eta l}} = \frac{{pr_1^4}}{{8\eta l}} + \frac{{\pi pr_2^4}}{{8\eta l}}$
$ \Rightarrow {r^4} = r_1^4 + r_2^4$
$\therefore r = {\left( {r_1^4 + r_2^4} \right)^{1/4}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Rate of Flow of Fluid through a Combination of Capillary Tubes

question: A metal sphere of radius ' $$R$$ ' and density ' $$\rho_{1}$$ ' is dropped in a liquid of density ' $$\sigma$$ ' moves with terminal velocity ' $$v$$ '. Another metal sphere of same radius and density ' $$\rho_{2}$$ ' is dropped in the same liquid, its terminal velocity will be
explanation:
The terminal velocity of the sphere
$v = \frac{2}{9}{R^2}\frac{{\left( {{\rho _1} - \sigma } \right)g}}{\eta }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Here $R$ = radius of sphere,
$$\rho_{1}=$$ density of sphere
and $$\sigma=$$ density of liquid
Now, if density of metal sphere is changed to $$\rho_{2}$$, then terminal velocity
${v_2} = \frac{2}{9}\frac{{{R^2}\left( {{\rho _2} - \sigma } \right)g}}{\eta }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Divide eq.(1) and (2), we get
$$\dfrac{v_{2}}{v}=\dfrac{\left(\rho_{2}-\sigma\right)}{\left(\rho_{1}-\sigma\right)} \text { or } v_{2}=\dfrac{v\left(\rho_{2}-\sigma\right)}{\left(\rho_{1}-\sigma\right)}$$
Hence the terminal velocity is $$\dfrac{v\left(\rho_{2}-\sigma\right)}{\left(\rho_{1}-\sigma\right)}$$
subject: Physics

question: A small metal sphere of mass $$M$$ and density $$d_{1}$$, when dropped in a jar filled with liquid moves with terminal velocity after sometime. The viscous force acting on the sphere is $$\left(d_{2}=\right.$$ density of liquid and $g = $ gravitational acceleration)
explanation:
The motion of metal sphere is shown as
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955661/preptracker/questions/q_37599_explanation_1764955660654.png
Where, $$F_{B}=$$ Buoyant force and
$$F_{v}=$$ viscous force
At terminal velocity, the net force on the sphere is zero
$$W=F_{B}+F_{v}$$
$$\Rightarrow m g=d_{2} V g+F_{v}$$
$$=d_{2} \dfrac{M}{d_{1}} g+F_{v}$$
$$\therefore F_{v}=M g\left(1-\dfrac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}\right)$$
subject: Physics

question: A spherical ball is dropped in a long column of a highly viscous liquid. The curve in the graph shown, which represents the speed of the ball (\[v\]) as a function of time ( \[t\]) is:
explanation:
Initially speed is zero, then increases & after some time it becomes constant.
Acceleration (slope of $v/t$ curve) of ball first decreases and after some time it becomes zero.
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Machine parts are jammed in winter

Reason: The viscosity of lubricant used in machine parts increase at low temperature.
explanation: Viscosities of fluids depend on temperature, increasing for gases and decreasing for liquids as the temperature is increased. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The viscosity of liquid increases with rise of tempature.


Reason: Viscosity of a liquid is the property of the liquid by virtue of which it opposes the relative motion among its different layers.
explanation: When the temperature of the liquid increases distance between molecules increases then cohesive force between liquid molecules decreases hence viscosity decreases. It is the property that opposes relative motion between adjacent layers. So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Terminal velocity is same as the critical velocity


Reason: The constant velocity of fall of a body through a viscous fluid is called terminal velocity.
explanation: Terminal velocity and critical velocity are not same. Critical velocity is the velocity below which the flow of liquid is streamline. So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
When non ideal fluids flow, there is no loss of energy due to friction.
Different layers of the fluid do not exert forces on each other.
explanation: In the flow of fluids, some energy does get lost due to internal friction. This arises due to the fact that in a fluid flow, the different layers of the fluid flow with different velocities. These layers exert frictional forces on each other resulting in a loss of energy. So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
I. Viscous force is proportional to the velocity of the object.
II. Viscous force is a dragging force which acts opposite to the direction of motion.
III. Viscous force depends on viscosity $$\eta$$ of the fluids and radius $$r$$ of the sphere.
Which of the following statements(s) is/are correct?
explanation: $$f=6 \pi \eta r v$$. So option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: A tall cylinder is filled with viscous oil. A round pebble is dropped from the top with zero initial velocity. From the plot shown in figure, indicate the one that represents the velocity $$(v)$$ of the pebble as function of time $$(t)$$.
explanation: When the pebble is dropped from the top of a tall cylinder, filled with viscous oil the pebble acquire terminal velocity (i.e., constant velocity) after some time.
subject: Physics

question: A container is filled with non-viscous fluid of density ' $$\rho$$ '. Initially, the entire system is at rest. Now, the container starts rotating with a constant angular velocity $$\omega$$ about its vertical axis. Choose the appropriate plot from below which depicts the shape of free surface of the fluid after a long time.
explanation: As the liquid is non-viscous the rotation of container will not have any effect on the liquid.
subject: Physics

question: A body is dropped in a viscous fluid. Predict the correct graph between acceleration \[vs\] time.
explanation: The acceleration of the body is maximum initially and decreases to zero when the body reaches terminal velocity.
subject: Physics

question: Three horizontal capillary tubes of same radii and lengths ${L_1},\,{L_2}$ and ${L_3}$ are fitted side by side to the wall of a tank that is filled with water. The length of a single capillary tube of same radius that can replace the three tubes such that the rate of flow of water through the single tube equals the combined rate of flow through the three tubes is
explanation:
The rate of flow of liquid is
$$\begin{aligned}& Q=Q_{1}+Q_{2}+Q_{3} \\& \dfrac{\pi P r^{4}}{8 \eta L}=\dfrac{\pi P r^{4}}{8 \eta L_{1}}+\dfrac{\pi P r^{4}}{8 \eta L_{2}}+\dfrac{\pi P r^{4}}{8 \eta L_{3}} \\& \dfrac{1}{L}=\dfrac{1}{L_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{L_{2}}+\dfrac{1}{L_{3}} \\& L=\dfrac{L_{1} L_{2} L_{3}}{L_{1} L_{2}+L_{2} L_{3}+L_{3} L_{1}}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Rate of Flow of Fluid through a Combination of Capillary Tubes

question: Two capillaries of same length and radii in the ratio 1: 2 are connected in series. A liquid flows through them in streamlined condition. If the pressure across the two extreme ends of the combination is $2\,m$ of water, the pressure difference across first capillary is
explanation:
Given, $$l_{1}=l_{2}=l$$ and $$\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$$
As the tubes are connected in series.
$$\begin{aligned}& Q=\dfrac{\pi P_{1} r_{1}^{4}}{8 \eta l}=\dfrac{\pi P_{2} r_{2}^{4}}{8 \eta l} \Rightarrow \dfrac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{4}=16 \\& \Rightarrow P_{1}=16 P_{2}\end{aligned}$$
Since both tubes are connected in series, hence pressure difference across combination,
$$P=P_{1}+P_{2} \Rightarrow 2=P_{1}+\dfrac{P_{1}}{16} \Rightarrow P_{1}=\dfrac{32}{17}=1.88 m$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Rate of Flow of Fluid through a Combination of Capillary Tubes

question: A small sphere falls from rest in a viscous liquid. Due to friction, heat is produced. The rate of production of heat depends on the radius as (At terminal velocity).
explanation:
Viscous force on a falling sphere in a liquid $$F=6 \pi \eta r v_{t}$$ where $$v_{t}=\dfrac{(2) r^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{9 \eta}$$ is terminal velocity, $$\rho=$$ density of sphere, $$\sigma=$$ density of liquid.
Rate of production of heat $$=F v_{t}$$
$$\begin{gathered}=6 \pi \eta r\left[\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta}\right]^{2}=\dfrac{8}{27} \dfrac{\pi g^{2}(\rho-\sigma)^{2}}{\eta} r^{5} \\\Rightarrow \dfrac{d Q}{d t} \propto r^{5}\end{gathered}$$
subject: Physics

question: Two metal spheres are falling through a liquid of density $2 \times {10^3}\,kg/{m^3}$ with the same uniform speed. The material density of sphere 1 and sphere 2 are $8 \times {10^3}\,kg/{m^3}$ and $11 \times {10^3}\,kg/{m^3}$ respectively. The ratio of their radii is
explanation:
The terminal velocity of the spherical body of radius $$R$$, density $$\rho$$ falling through a liquid of density $$\sigma$$ is given by
$$v_{t}=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{R^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta}$$
Where $$\eta$$ is the coefficient of viscosity of the liquid
$$\therefore v_{t_{1}}=\dfrac{2 R_{1}^{2}\left(\rho_{1}-\sigma\right) g}{9 \eta} \text { and } v_{t_{2}}=\dfrac{2 R_{2}^{2}\left(\rho_{2}-\sigma\right) g}{9 \eta}$$
According to the given problem, $$v_{t_{1}}=v_{t_{2}}$$
$$R_{1}^{2}\left(\rho_{1}-\sigma\right)=R_{2}^{2}\left(\rho_{2}-\sigma\right) \text { or } \dfrac{R_{1}^{2}}{R_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{\rho_{2}-\sigma}{\rho_{1}-\sigma}$$
Substituting the given values, we get
$$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{R_{1}^{2}}{R_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{\left(11 \times 10^{3}-2 \times 10^{3}\right)}{\left(8 \times 10^{3}-2 \times 10^{3}\right)}=\dfrac{3}{2} \\& \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: When a ball is released from rest in a very long column of viscous liquid, its downward acceleration is ' $$a$$ ' (just after release). Then its acceleration when it has acquired two third of the maximum velocity:
explanation:
When the ball is just released, the net force on ball is $$m a=m g-F_{b}$$
The terminal velocity ' $$v_{t}$$ ' of the ball is attained when net force on the ball is zero.
$$m g=F_{b}+6 \pi \eta r v_{t}$$
When the ball acquires $$\dfrac{2}{3} \mathrm{rd}$$ of its maximum velocity $$v_{f}$$
$$\begin{aligned}& m a^{\prime}=m g-F_{b}-6 \pi \eta r\left(\dfrac{2 v_{t}}{3}\right) \\& m a^{\prime}=m a-\dfrac{2}{3}(m a) \\& a^{\prime}=\dfrac{a}{3}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform solid sphere of relative density 5 is released in water filled in a long vertical tube. Its terminal velocity achieved is $$V$$. If another uniform solid sphere of same material but double the radius is released in the same water then the terminal velocity achieved will be.
explanation:
Initially the terminal velocity $$V$$ of sphere of radius $$a$$ is
${W_{eff{\text{ }}}} = 6\pi \eta aV\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\left( {{W_{eff{\text{ }}}} = {\text{ weight - buoyant force }}} \right)$
As the radius is doubled, mass is increased to 8 times and new terminal velocity will be
$8{W_{eff{\text{ }}}} = 6\pi \eta 2a{V^\prime }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2) $$V^{\prime}=4 V$$
subject: Physics

question: A marble of mass $$x$$ and diameter $$2 r$$ is gently released in a tall cylinder containing honey. If the marble displaces mass $$y( < x)$$ of the liquid, then the terminal velocity is proportional to
explanation:
If $$v$$ is the terminal velocity, then the force,
$$\begin{aligned}& x g-y g=6 \pi \eta r v \\& \Rightarrow v=\dfrac{(x-y)}{r} \dfrac{g}{6 \pi \eta} \\& \Rightarrow v \propto \dfrac{(x-y)}{r}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: Eight drops of water, each of radius $2\,mm$ are falling through air at a terminal velocity of $8\,cm\,{s^{ - 1}}$. If they coalesce to form a single drop, then the terminal velocity of combined drop will be
explanation:
Let the radius of bigger drop is $R$ and that of smaller drop is $$r$$ then
$\frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3} = 8 \times \frac{4}{3} \times \pi {r^3}$
${\text{ or }}\quad R = 2r\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Terminal velocity, $$v \propto r^{2}$$
$\therefore \quad \frac{{{v^\prime }}}{v} = \frac{{{R^2}}}{{{r^2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{2r}}{r}} \right)^2} = 4$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {v^\prime } = 4v = 4 \times 8 = 32\,cm\,{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: Spherical balls of radius ' $$R$$ ' are falling in a viscous fluid of viscosity ' $$\eta$$ ' with a velocity ' $$v$$ '. The retarding viscous force acting on the spherical ball is
explanation:
From Stoke's law
Viscous force $$F=6 \pi \eta R v$$
hence $F$ is directly proportional to radius & velocity.

options.text: Inversely proportional to both radius ' $$R$$ ' and velocity ' $$v$$ '
Directly proportional to both radius ' $$R$$ ' and velocity ' $$v$$ '
Directly proportional to ' $$R$$ ' but inversely proportional to ' $$v$$ '
Inversely proportional to ' $$R$$ ' but Directly proportional to velocity ' $$v$$ '

subject: Physics

question: A thin plate of large area $$A$$ is placed midway in a gap of height $$h$$ filled with oil of viscosity $$\eta_{0}$$ and the plate is pulled at constant velocity $$v$$ by applying drag force on the plate. If a lighter oil of viscosity $$\eta$$ is then substituted in the gap, it is found that for the velocity $$v$$, and the same drag force as previous case the plate is located unsymmetrically in the gap but parallel to the walls. Find $$\eta$$ in terms of distance from nearer wall to the plane $$y$$.
explanation:
Case I:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955999/preptracker/questions/q_37278_explanation_1764955998900.png
Here viscous force acts on the plate on both sides
Drag force $$=F_{1}+F_{2}$$
$$=\left[\eta_{0} \dfrac{v}{h / 2}+\eta_{0} \dfrac{v}{h / 2}\right] A=\dfrac{4 \eta_{0} v A}{h}$$
Case II:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956000/preptracker/questions/q_37278_explanation_1764955999712.png
Drag force on the plate
$$=\left[\eta \dfrac{v}{h-y}+\eta \dfrac{v}{y}\right] A=\dfrac{\eta v h A}{y(h-y)}$$
As drag forces are equal
$$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{4 \eta_{0} v A}{h}=\dfrac{\eta v h A}{y(h-y)} \\& \Rightarrow \eta=\dfrac{4 \eta_{0} y}{h}\left[1-\dfrac{y}{h}\right]\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: The space between two large horizontal metal plates, $6\,\,cm$ apart, is filled with a liquid of viscosity $0.8\,N/{m^2}$. A thin plate of surface area $0.01\,\,{m^2}$ is moved parallel to the length of the plate such that the plate is at a distance of $2\,m$ from one of the plates and $4\,cm$ from the other. If the plate moves with a constant speed of $1\,m{s^{ - 1}}$, then
explanation:
The profile of the velocity of fluid is as shown in the figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954872/preptracker/questions/q_37274_explanation_1764954872188.png
The velocity of the layer of fluid, which is in contact with metal plates (fixed), is zero. As we move towards the centre from either plate, the velocity of the layer of fluid increases and it becomes maximum at the location of moving plate.

options.text: The layers of the fluid, which is in contact with the moving plate, is having the maximum velocity
The layer of the fluid, which is having the maximum velocity, is lying mid-way between the plates
The layer of the fluid, which is in contact with the two large plates, moving with the maximum velocity.
The layer of the fluid, which is in contact with the moving plate and is on the side of nearer plate, is moving with the maximum velocity

subject: Physics

question: A tube of radius $$r$$ and length $$l$$ is connected in series with another tube of radius $$\dfrac{r}{2}$$ and length $$\dfrac{l}{8}$$. If the pressure difference across the tubes taken together is $P$, the pressure difference across the two tubes separately
explanation:
The rate of liquid flow is
$$Q=\dfrac{\pi \operatorname{Pr}^{4}}{8 \eta l}$$
Let $$P_{1} \& P_{2}$$ are the pressure difference across the tubes.
$P = {P_1} + {P_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
As the flow of liquid is same in both tubes
${Q_1} = {Q_2}$
$\frac{{\pi {P_1}{r^4}}}{{8\eta l}} = \frac{{\pi {P_2}{{\left( {\frac{r}{2}} \right)}^4}}}{{8\eta \frac{l}{8}}} \Rightarrow {P_1} = \frac{{{P_2}}}{2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From eq's (1) & (2)
$$P_{1}=\dfrac{P}{3} \& P_{2}=\dfrac{2 P}{3}$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Rate of Flow of Fluid through a Combination of Capillary Tubes

question: What is the velocity $$v$$ of a metallic ball of radius $$r$$ falling in a tank of liquid at the instant when its acceleration is one-half that of a freely falling body? (The densities of metal and of liquid are $$\rho$$ and $$\sigma$$ respectively, and the viscosity of the liquid is $$\eta$$ )
explanation:
Given that $$a=\dfrac{g}{2}$$
$$\begin{aligned}& m a=m g-F_{b}-f \\& m \dfrac{g}{2}=m g-F_{b}-f \\& m \dfrac{g}{2}=F_{b}+f\end{aligned}$$
$$\begin{aligned}& \rho V \dfrac{g}{2}=\sigma V g+6 \pi \eta r v \\& v=\dfrac{V g\left(\dfrac{\rho}{2}-\sigma\right)}{6 \pi \eta r}=\dfrac{r^{2} g(\rho-2 \sigma)}{9 \eta}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: When a body falls in air, the resistance of air depends to a great extent on the shape of the body, 3 different shapes are given. Identify the combination of air resistances which truly represents the physical situation. (The cross sectional areas are the same).
explanation: A stream lined body has less resistance due to air. Here cigar shaped body will have least resistance.
subject: Physics

question: The terminal speed attained by an aluminium sphere of radius $1\,mm$ falling through water at $20^\circ {\text{ }}C$ will be close to (Assume laminar flow, specific gravity of $$A l=2.7$$ and $$\eta_{\text {water }}=8 \times 10^{-4}$$ )
explanation:
Terminal speed, $$v=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta}$$
Where $$\rho$$ and $$\sigma$$ are the densities of spherical body and medium respectively, $$r$$ is the radius of the spherical body and $$\eta$$ is the coefficient of viscosity of the medium.
Here, $r = 1\,mm = {10^{ - 3}}$
Specific gravity of $$A l=2.7$$
Density of $Al,\rho = 2.7 \times {10^3}\,kg/{m^3}$
Density of water, $\sigma = {10^3}\,kg/{m^3}$
$\eta = 8 \times {10^{ - 4}},g = 9.8\,m/{s^2}$
$\therefore \quad v = \frac{{2{{\left( {{{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)}^2} \times \left( {2.7 \times {{10}^3} - {{10}^3}} \right) \times 9.8}}{{9 \times 8 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}} = 4.6\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: A thin plate of area $2\,\,{m^2}$ is moving between two fixed parallel surfaces as shown in the figure. The liquid present on both sides of the plate is same and has coefficient of viscosity $$\eta=0.01$$ poise. Find the external force require to make the plate to move with constant velocity $2\,m/s$.
explanation:
As the liquid is present on both sides of the plate the viscous force on the plate by both the liquids is
$$f=\dfrac{\eta A v}{d_{1}}+\dfrac{\eta A v}{d_{2}}$$
Given that $$\eta=0.01$$ Poise $ = {10^{ - 3}}\frac{{Ns}}{{{m^2}}}$
$A = 2\,{m^2},v = 2\,m/s$
${d_1} = 0.5\,m,{d_2} = 0.1\,m$
$f = 12 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,N$
subject: Physics

question: A cubical block of side ' $$a$$ ' and density ' $$\rho$$ ' slides over a fixed inclined plane with constant velocity ' $$v$$ '. There is a thin film of viscous fluid of thickness ' $$t$$ ' between the plane and the block. Then the coefficient of viscosity of the thin film will be: (Acceleration due to gravity is $$g$$ )
explanation:
As the block moves with constant velocity
Viscous force $$=m g \sin \theta$$
$$\begin{gathered}\therefore \quad \eta A \dfrac{v}{t}=m g \sin \theta \\\eta a^{2} \dfrac{v}{t}=a^{3} \rho g \sin \theta \\\eta=\dfrac{t \rho g \sin \theta a}{v}\end{gathered}$$
subject: Physics

A Terminal velocity

question: A volume $V$ of viscous liquid flows per unit time due to a pressure head $$\Delta P$$ along a pipe of diameter $D$ and length $L$. Instead of this pipe a set of 4 pipes each of diameter $$\dfrac{D}{2}$$ and length $$2 L$$ is connected to the same pressure head $$\Delta P$$. Now, the volume of the liquid flowing per unit time is
explanation:
Given that
$$\begin{aligned}& V=\dfrac{\pi \Delta P D^{4}}{64 \eta L} \quad\left(r=\dfrac{D}{2}\right) \\& V^{\prime}=4\left[\dfrac{\pi \Delta P D^{4}}{8 \times 64 \eta 2 L}\right]=\dfrac{V}{4}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Rate of Flow of Fluid through a Combination of Capillary Tubes

question: The formula for the resistance of a fluid is
explanation:
The rate of liquid flow is
$Q = \frac{{\pi \Delta {{\Pr }^4}}}{{8\eta l}} = \frac{{\Delta P}}{{\left( {\frac{{8\eta l}}{{\pi {r^4}}}} \right)}}$
By comparing the above equation with ohm's law $$i=\dfrac{\Delta V}{R}$$ then the resistance offered to the fluid is $$\quad R=\dfrac{8 \eta \ell}{\pi r^{4}}$$
subject: Physics

question: Water flows in a streamlined manner through a capillary tube of radius $$a$$, the pressure difference being $$P$$ and the rate of flow $$Q$$. If the radius is reduced to $$a / 2$$ and the pressure increased to $$2 P$$, the rate of flow becomes
explanation:
$$V=\dfrac{\pi \Delta P r^{4}}{8 \eta l} \quad \therefore V \propto \Delta \operatorname{Pr}^{4}$$
[ $$\eta$$ and $$l$$ are constants]
$$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad \dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{4}=2 \times\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{4}=\dfrac{1}{8} \\& \therefore V_{2}=\dfrac{Q}{8}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: Two identical spherical drops of water are falling (vertically downwards) through air with a steady velocity of $5\,\,cm/\sec $. If both the drops coalesce (combine) to form a new spherical drop, the terminal velocity of the new drop will be (neglect buoyant force on the drops).
explanation:
When two drops of radius $r$ each combine to form a big drop, the radius of big drop will be given by
$$\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=\dfrac{4 \pi}{3} r^{2}+\dfrac{4 \pi}{3} r^{3}$$

$$\text { or } R^{3}=2 r^{3} \text { or } R=2^{1 / 3} r$$
Now $$\quad \dfrac{v_{R}}{v_{r}}=\left(\dfrac{R}{r}\right)^{2}=2^{\frac{2}{3}}=4^{\frac{1}{3}}$$
$\therefore \quad {v_R} = 5 \times {4^{1/3}}\,cm/s$
subject: Physics

question: A rain drop of radius $1.5\,mm$, experiences a drag force $$F=\left(2 \times 10^{-5} v\right) N$$, while falling through air from a height $2\,km$, with a velocity $$v$$. The terminal velocity of the rain drop will be nearly ( use $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
When the rain drop reaches terminal velocity then
$$\begin{gathered}F=2 \times 10^{-5} v=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \rho g \\\Rightarrow 2 \times 10^{-5} v=\dfrac{4}{3} \times \dfrac{22}{7} \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{3} \times 10^{3} \times 10\end{gathered}$$
On solving, $$v=7.07 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \approx 7 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$$
subject: Physics

question: Two rain drops reach the earth with different terminal velocities having ratio $$9: 4$$. Then the ratio of their volume is
explanation: $$\begin{gathered}v_{t}=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{g r^{2}[\rho-\sigma]}{\eta} \Rightarrow v_{t} \propto r^{2} \propto\left(\mathrm{V}^{1 / 3}\right)^{2} \propto \mathrm{V}^{2 / 3} \\\dfrac{v_{t_{1}}}{v_{t_{2}}}=\left(\dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}\right)^{2 / 3} \\\dfrac{9}{4}=\left(\dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}\right)^{2 / 3} \Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\dfrac{27}{8}\end{gathered}$$
subject: Physics

question: A small steel ball of mass $$m$$ and radius $$r$$ is falling under gravity through a viscous liquid of coefficient of viscocity $$\eta$$. If $$g$$ is the value of acceleration due to gravity, then the terminal velocity of the ball is proportional to (ignore buoyancy)
explanation:
As the steel ball is small therefore upthrust can be neglected.
Now, $$6 \pi \eta r v_{0}=m g$$ or $$v_{0} \propto \dfrac{m g}{\eta r}$$
subject: Physics

question: A sphere of mass $$M$$ and radius $$R$$ is falling in a viscous fluid. The terminal velocity attained by the object will be proportional to:
explanation:
The terminal velocity of a sphere of radius $$R$$ moving in a liquid of coefficient of viscosity
$$\eta \text { is } \quad \Rightarrow v=\dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{R^{2}(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta}$$
Where $$\rho$$ - density of the sphere
$$\sigma$$-density of the liquid
$$\Rightarrow v \propto R^{2}$$
subject: Physics

question: A rain drop of radius $0.3\,\,mm$ falling vertically downwards in air has a terminal velocity of 1 $m/s$. The viscosity of air is $$18 \times 10^{-5}$$ poise. The viscous force on the drop is
explanation:

$ = 6\pi \eta rv = 6\pi \times 18 \times {10^{ - 5}} \times 0.03 \times 100$
$ = 101.73 \times {10^{ - 4}}dyn\quad \left( {dyn = 1g\,cm/{s^2}} \right)$

question: The viscous force on a sphere moving in a liquid depends on
explanation: Viscosity force on a moving sphere in a liquid is $$f=6 \pi \eta r v$$

question: A square plate of $0.1\,\,m$ side moves parallel to a second plate with a velocity of $0.1\,m/s$, both plates being immersed in water. If the viscous force is $0.002\,\,N$ and the coefficient of viscocity is 0.01 poise, distance between the plates in $$m$$ is
explanation:
$A = {(0.1)^2} = 0.01\,{m^2}$,
$$\eta=0.01$$ Poise $$=0.001$$ decapoise (M.K.S.unit)
$dv = 0.1\,m/s$ and $F = 0.002\,N$
$F = \eta A\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}$
$\therefore dx = \frac{{\eta Adv}}{F} = \frac{{0.001 \times 0.01 \times 0.1}}{{0.002}} = 0.0005\,m$
subject: Physics

question: If the shearing stress between the horizontal layers of water in a river is $1.5\,milli$ newton $/{m^2}$ and $$\eta_{\text {water }}=1 \times 10^{-3}$$ $pa.s$, The velocity gradient is ${s^{ - 1}}$
explanation:
The velocity gradient is
$$\dfrac{d v}{d x}=\dfrac{F}{A \eta}=1.5 s^{-1}$$

question: A container filled with viscous liquid is moving vertically downwards with constant speed $$4 v_{0}$$. At the instant shown, a sphere of radius $$r$$ is moving vertically downwards (in liquid) has speed $$v_{0}$$. The coefficient of viscosity is $$\eta$$. There is no relative motion between the liquid and the container. Then at the shown instant, the magnitude of viscous force acting on sphere is
explanation:
Relative to liquid, the velocity of sphere is $$3 v_{0}$$ upwards.
$$\therefore$$ Viscous force on sphere $$=6 \pi \eta r 3 v_{0}$$ downward
$$=18 \pi \eta r v_{0} \text { downward }$$

question: The velocity of a ball of mass ' $$m$$ ' density ' $$d_{1}$$ ' when dropped in a container filled with glycerin of density ' $$d_{2}$$ ' becomes constant after sometime. The viscous force acting on the ball will be
explanation:
Viscous force $$=$$ Weight - Force of buoyancy
$$\begin{gathered}\Rightarrow f_{v}=m g-F_{B}=m g-d_{2} v g \\=m g\left[1-\dfrac{d_{2} v g}{m g}\right]=m g\left[1-\dfrac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}\right]\end{gathered}$$

question: Two spheres of the same material, but of radii $$R$$ and $$3 R$$ are allowed to fall vertically downwards through a liquid of density $$\sigma$$. The ratio of their terminal velocities is

question: The velocity of the surface layer of water in a river of depth $10\,m$ is $5\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The shearing stress between the surface layer and the bottom layer is (Coefficient of viscosity of water, $\eta  = {10^{ - 3}}SI$ units)
explanation:
Here, $\eta = {10^{ - 3}}N{m^{ - 2}}\,s,v = 5\,m{s^{ - 1}};l = 10\,m$
Strain rate $$=\dfrac{v}{l}$$
Coefficient of viscosity, $$\eta=\dfrac{\text { Shearing stress }}{\text { Strain rate }}$$
$$\therefore$$ Shearing stress $$=\eta \times$$ Strain rate
$ = \frac{{\left( {{{10}^{ - 3}}N{m^{ - 2}}\,s} \right)\left( {5\,m{s^{ - 1}}} \right)}}{{(10\,m)}} = 0.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}N{m^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics

question:
Viscous force is similar to friction in solids, but viscous force
I. Is independent of area but friction depends on area
II. Is temperature dependent while friction force between solids depends upon normal reaction
III. Is velocity dependent while friction is velocity independent
explanation:
Viscosity of a liquid depends on temperature. Viscous force $$f=\eta A \dfrac{v}{d}$$
Viscous force depends on area, separation between two layers and relative velocity between two layers.
Fricition force between two solids $$f_{K}=\mu_{K} N$$ depends on only normal force.
subject: Physics

question: The dimensions of coefficient of viscosity is
explanation:
The viscous force on a sphere is
